The bottom line is VR with Meta is a huge setback and I am now looking into steam VR or other versions. The store is a bit of a mess compared to the PC app version as being able to list simply what you own in your library seems absent.

text:
I'm very close to getting my first VR headset and this one looks like the best value. I almost pulled the trigger on a Quest 2 a few years ago after a friend had had a Quest 2 for a while and he advised me to definitely get the add-on battery strap, not for the extra battery life (although that's always nice of course) but because the weight of the extra battery at the back of the strap made the Quest 2 way more comfortable to wear because it balanced the weight better.

I'm wondering what people's advice is for the Quest 3 when it comes to straps. Is the basic one that comes with the headset good enough or, if getting a strap upgrade, does the Quest 3 also benefit from significantly better weight distribution/balance with the Elite rechargeable strap or does the basic Elite strap (without rechargeable battery) add enough extra comfort to make it not worth going with the battery version (if all you care about is comfort not extended battery life)?

Alternatively, if someone does decide they want extended battery life, are there any downsides with the Elite rechargeable strap e.g. does it actually disturb the weight distribution of the basic headset and make it feel less stable on one's head?

text:
If anyone new is looking to buy this, dont. For 600 euros, the quality is idiotic. Mine for example, came with a broken face protection. I decided to try it despite that before replacing to new one. Half an hour in, still can't even test it. After initial setup, you will be asked for a mandatory installation of some fishy meta app, which apparently only required to well, I don't even know what is it required for. Everyone said it's only for initial registration then can be uninstalled.
So I register an account. It immediately gets blocked. Start solving 10 captchas. Then it asks for face photo to unblock an account which I created 5 minutes ago xD Like seriously, wtf?!@?!. Do I also have to take a photo of my ID? Maybe bank account too? And then I will be granted right to use the damn TV on my face? xDD WTF?! And that app supposedly is not even required, so why the hell do I have to go through hell just to register on some fishy app????

I immediately know it is to be refunded and I will buy other product, probably with less marketing and better quality instead.

What stops me from getting a meta quest is the fact that the batteries are sealed inside the headset. They have continued to improve the passthrough and virtual desktop resolution. Both are very good now. My one big complaint is that I wish it had a display port input for uncompressed PCVR display. I will say that whatever compression artifacts there are don't bother me. I barely notice them and I don't think it should be a deal-breaker for anyone. I also understand that Meta is trying to push the standalone side of VR gaming, and that is where their real money is made. However, I still think they'd make a lot of PCVR users happy with the addition of a simple port. Maybe in the Q4 or the Q Pro 2?

text:
Whether or not the Quest 3, or any VR headset is worth buying is heavily dependent on the individual. For some the immersion in VR games will make it worth while. For others like me, it is worth while solely for its awesome ability to act as a multi-monitor setup for both work and PC gaming.

During the day I have a monitor open for work, and one or two monitors to the side for my two personal computers, one Linux and another for WIndows gaming. Other windows for email, social media, music and the like are scattered around me as they're needed. Although I would love to have a 4k monitor experience, being surrounded by multiple 1080p, and smaller for less content demanding apps over an array of three computers and the headsets applications is amazing. Whereas before my content were forced into specific locations on two monitors due to physical constraints, the content I see can now be moved around the way you'd move pieces of paper around on a desk to have relevant content center focus and everything else close by when needed.

Although I haven't played any VR games for a few months, my Quest 3 is in daily use, some times up to 14 hours a day. I got into the headset at 09h30 this morning, and except for a few well timed breaks it has been on my head the whole time, with me typing this comment on a giant floating screen at 23h00. It's great for consuming media while washing the dishes, or cooking.

The big problems at the moment that put a lot of people off working in VR such as comfort and eye strain are complex challenges that I don't believe the headset manufacturers quite know how to solve yet. I use reduced prescription contact lenses that moves the focal point to close to infinity to help with eye strain and I have a BoboVR headstrap for balanced all day comfort and of course battery life. At times I wear the headset without the facial interface so I have peripheral vision and less pressure on my face. It is still not perfect, just the best compromise I could come up with.

text:
The Quest 3 is such an unbelievable piece of trash.
I got mine about 6 months ago and the only thing this piece of sh*t is, is trouble.

- Every single update ends in an update loop where i need to fiddle around with the recovery menu just to get it back up booting.
- Room boundaries do not work or at least no reliable. Furniture often changes position and one time i even had the entire room breaking apart (the floor being non existing, walls being torn apart and so on).
- Performance in some applications is worse than with the Quest 2, because yeah, they upped the screen resolution significantly but failed to increase the hardware specs accordingly.
- Battery power is just laughable, it can't even last a 1 hour session. Charging while using is also a no go because then it get's uncomfortable hot on the face.

All in all the Quest 2 was a much more rounded product. I've never had a single problem with the Quest 2 whereas the Quest 3 gave me problems from minute one.
The device also got replaced one time so it is not a faulty product.

It's just a beta version of a most likely upcoming product and we got to pay for it.

text: Honestly I found I use my Quest 3 WAAAAAAAYYYYY more than I used the Quest 2. Its just more comfortable to wear. The Quest 2 sticks out the front much more and while it is technically lighter, that front loading makes it feel heavier. A good headstrap (I use the BoboVR M3 pro) and it is even better and I can wear it for hours. Plus the lovely pancake lenses and their huge sweet spot just means getting it clear and comfortable are WAY easier. Its just a vast improvement all around IMO and while I would look at the Quest 2 sometimes and end up going "Naw, its too much hassle to put on and get adjusted", with the Quest 3 its like "Oh yeah this is just easy on and off, time to jump into VR for a half hour or so". Honestly I have used it basically every single day since I got it and with Asgard's Wrath to getting yet another Quest 3 upgrade, that doesn't look like it will change anytime soon.
author: @Mandrake42

text: There is a cool program called Quest Game Optimizer as well that has a whole bunch of presents for Meta games that increases the games resolution and performance to take advantage of the Quest 3's processor. Its most effective on older games as the difference is less noticeable in new flagship titles like Asgard's Wrath 2 and Assassin's Creed Nexus where they already have Quest 3 upgrades that push the hardware, I have really noticed the difference using it for things like Real VR Fishing, Galaxy's Edge and other games that have yet to receive any specific Quest 3 updates. Its well worth it so long as you are okay with using Sidequest to sideload third party programs.\

text: Honestly I think if your aren't into gaming, VR might be a waste of your money. Sure there are other things you can do like watching 360 videos or giant 2d screens, multitask web browsing or social features like virtual chatrooms, but to me at least gaming is the one thing that is seamless convenient and unique in VR. All the other stuff is more novelty, like yeah it's cool to have a bunch of virtual monitors to to work on, but your also wearing a heavy pair of goggles on your face with heat blowing all over your head and a limited battery life, and I'd rather just have 2 monitors and a PC than do all that. But gaming is where I can't get the same experience traditionally, and it's not only visually interesting but the controls, movement, and mechanics of games are a vast world of possibilities in VR. If you ever get the chance to try one out go for it but I wouldn't spend $500 dollars for the non gaming features of VR, maybe check back when they come out with the meta AI glasses they are talking about that might be more useful and less cumbersome for someone who doesn't game

text:
Keeping my eye on the price. If it goes on a sale, then I'll probably take the plunge. The wifi thing is a little bothersome, but honestly, the pancake lenses and the removal of the sensor rings in the controllers are the things I'm most interested in. Having only a very small area where the image is clear is really, REALLY obnoxious, and I've bashed the controller rings against a wall I didn't know I was so close to. To their credit, they've held up amazingly well, but it'd be nice not to deal with them. All in all, I'm interested, but not $500 interested.

Also, Walkabout mini-golf. I originally wanted the VR for things like zombie shooters and Blade and Sorcery, but I was absolutely not prepared for how many hours of my life I'd lose to Walkabout. This game doesn't seem to get a ton of a fanfare in the VR world, but I'm hopelessly addicted a year after my initial purchase. It doesn't hurt to have a core group of friends that play either. We do a game or two on Sunday's, and sometimes fill the room almost to it's capacity of 8, and have a ton of fun with it. Seriously, check that game out. It's wonderful.

text:
You have to keep in mind that the headset has vignetting because of the pancake lenses, basically the rim of your vision appears to darken rather than stay the same brightness like on the Quest 2. It can be jarring for some people, and you’ll probably need to increase the brightness as well, the screen isn’t as bright as the quest 2.

text:

There's definitely not a noticeable screen door effect, unless you're looking for it, but for detailed content or grids (moir patterns), sizing of your content may be affected, to where watching it on a 4K TV is far better.

author: @K.o.r.3.G.a.m.i.n.g_G.h.o.s.t

text:
Personally I came from a RiftS with Occulus/Meta, but I also have a Valve Index - Knuckles Controllers are still the best hands down but the Quest 3 controllers are built like tanks and lightweight, Tracking is much much Better than the RiftS was despite having better placed Camera array. Honestly LCD/Lenses I prefer the Quest 3 the pancake lenses just make that much more a difference, you aren't getting the blacks that the Index has, Its really hard to compare the 2 as the Quest 3 relies on compression versus a native render from the GPU, I wish the Quest 3 had USBtype C with DP support for the USB jack it would honestly make such a difference and a more fair comparable comparison because some games just don't Compress well, although some apps support the AV1 codec which makes a huge difference if you are using NVidia 4000 series. Still the image quality is real nice.
From the RIftS its a major step up, Index though is a bit more complicated, If you have the extra money to toss around I would say get the Quest 3 just because it has the ability to go anywhere. However I have a major feeling Valve is cooking in the kitchen again and basically we will get a superior standalone from Valve in the next couple years that will be a no compromises headset. Personally I think the speeds of Wifi 7 will be the key to wireless HMDs having a higher bandwidth allowing for less compression artifacting and image degradation while being more stable as well. I can see Virtualization in reverse using ARM chips to run X86/x64 applications at lower settings on the go, and probably Hybrid tracking.
I personally Meta is going nowhere for the forseeable future, even if the company closed shop Valve would buy them in a heartbeat and transition everything to their branding and such.

Only change I did is replace the original head strap with the BoboVR S3 Pro head strap. Besides better comfort, it provides me a battery which has larger capacity for playing even longer times with the Quest 3.
Hand controllers are only used for selecting menus and work very good. I do not do action VR games which swing them around all over the place. I am always sitting down playing racing games.
Thanks for your video. Take care.

Quest 3's XR2 Gen 2's GPU ≈3.4 TFLOPS. Aka almost but not quite as much raw compute as a PS4 Pro (≈4.2 TFLOPS) but with a WAAAAAAY faster CPU and a MUCH more modern GPU feature-set.

The one place traditional consoles are still WELL ahead of mobile hardware is in terms of memory bandwidth though (Quest 3 ≈90GB/s, base PS4 ≈176GB/s, although the former has much better memory compression tech meaning it's prolly more like ≈120GB/s vs 176GB/s in terms of real-world practical performance).
author: @Cooe.

text: on a comment made in verdict - most precisely tracked controllers.. does it really outperform Valve index with base stations?
author: @PettenVeevo

text:
No, but the optional upgrade fully self-tracked Touch Pro controllers do! 🤷 (Aka the controllers the Quest Pro ships with). They have absolutely ZERO tracking deadzones or occlusion issues, which even with outside-in Lighthouse tracking can still be an issue.

Sure, Lighthouse has a slight latency advantage by running tracking updates at 100Hz to Touch Pro's 60Hz, but IMO I'd MUCH rather have its guaranteed no deadzones or possibility for occlusion (and ability to be used ANYWHERE w/o external base stations) than Lighthouse's small latency advantage.

... Touch controllers also don't spontaneously break and fall apart even if you just accidentally look at them the wrong way ala Index's Knuckles, so there's that. 🤷

That's because there LITERALLY isn't anything that's indisputably better! 🤷

Even the $1000 for JUST the headset (aka no base stations or controllers, it's more like ≈$1500 w/ those) Bigscreen Beyond has significantly worse lenses (WAAAAAAY more glare & reflections than Meta's custom pancakes), a smaller FOV, no wireless support (even just wireless tethering, let ALONE full standalone capability), AND a lower refresh rate (90Hz at reduced Quest 2-ish res & just 75Hz at the full 2560x2560p res, vs up to 120Hz at whatever res the Q3 game/PC can handle).

text:
As of two weeks ago I now own the Quest 3. I had previously owned the Quest 2 but returned it because I ended up having far more compromises to get PC Link working correctly and could never figure out the nuance of starting it up so I could run it off of a first party Link Cable to my PC. Until I realized that I really didn't want to run a cable all of the time, because it limited my movement in the space I was playing in.

The Quest 3 pretty much improved the wireless Link experience, enhanced with the free Steam Link app through the Meta app store directly. No compromises and an excellent picture quality inside the goggles. The only things I need now are prescription lenses I can install so I no longer have to wear my glasses.

text:
ewwww used vr headsets? Be full of their sweat etc 🤢


Last time ever used anything "VR" was the Virtual Boy as a kid. Don't think need to say why the entire concept became a joke to me for years.

Watching friends in more recent years drop serious money on these big bulky things, hearing them complain about heat exhaustion and motion sickness, I continued to treat VR as a gimmicky, now expensive, joke.

Then, on a complete whim mainly cos wanted to try out that Batman game, I bought a Quest 3. Glad I waited all these years, cos yeah I know there's better visuals out there for them, and better tracking etc, but when your last frame of reference for the entire tech was a boondoggle thing Nintendo pushed out eons ago? I mean...it's the closest to living what Ready Player One describes I've ever seen. Took me less than an hour too with zero PCVR experience to get it going in Steam Link, blowing myself up in Elite Dangerous on my fleet carrier cos too distracted watching it fly by up top to see where I was going lol Good times.

It's been a solid value, and seamless experience for someone who knew nothing about modern VR a week ago. Excited now too for AR, longest time just assumed never really go anywhere but with way the Q3 is going, wild to think where this stuff might actually go irl applications within next couple decades.

text:
After a bit of time with the Quest 3 I like product but there's two major issues. Most of the games are poor after the novelty wears off. And... I get headaches from it after an hour or so.

The ratings system in their store is entirely useless. Even games with top ratings are often little more than buggy tech demos. Most are short and expensive for what you get.

There are a few gems but not nearly as many as reviews suggest. There is obviously a lot of cheating going on. Paid reviewers and bots etc.

The graphics are PS1/ PS2 level at best and qc is comparable to Android app level. If you are looking forward to running around cutting baddies down with a light-saber... forget it. Even the star wars games suck.

They really need to start making games that stand up on their own (not just vr novelty) or people will lose interest.

For now... PC VR is the way to go.

text:
My problem with this headset is:
I am a PCVR user. I don't have an overpowered PC so I can play stardew valley........ y'know?
That said, when I'm in the market for a VR, I want it to handle PCVR and anything beyond that is plain unnecessary and will never get used by someone like me.
This means the battery pack, the CPUs, the everything is all a waste of money.
Why can't we go back and revisit PCVR exclusively? Headsets could be lighter, cheaper, and you can actually get away with higher specs at the cost of not having all that extra unnecessary stuff also built in.
That's just my view point, personally, as I own a quest 2 and NEVER use it as a quest. It is exclusively just a wireless PCVR headset, except that because of quest link, barely even works half the time. I had to go buy external, aftermarket software, VirtualDesktop, to get a reliable connection. It's whack that I have to do that though when Quest Link is an advertised feature. It just simply doesn't fucking work half the time and I even went so far as getting a dedicated higher-end router specifically to host my quest's connection to PC.
That's insane to me.
Wireless is cool, I guess, but it just simply doesn't make up for all the issues I have with quest link software, plus, even wired it has the exact same connection issues.

It gets EVEN WORSE when you consider that I run a rx 7900xt GPU, WHICH to those of you that don't know, is not even SLIGHTLY supported by Quest Link, so since upgrading my GPU I've been unable to use the quest link AT ALL, let alone as unreliably as before with an rtx 3090.
It's completely assinine.

I don't care how good this headset is, Meta will not get anymore of my money as they have spat on me as a customer just about every chance they've gotten. I've never felt more ripped off by a supposedly professional corporation. I'd expect this kind of behavior out of some cheap china knock off scam service or something....... but not Meta.

Even before all that, the performance of Quest link straight up sucked.

It bothers me that you're giving this thing so much credit, Linus. As my entire experience with the quest 2 made me just feel ripped off so bad that I don't even want to give the pro or the 3 a chance.. because my problem was never with the headset or hardware, but with the software and Meta themself.

This is obnoxiously long for a youtube comment, but I have strong opinions on these products.

I'm soon going to purchase an Index, but I've been patiently waiting to see if something new/better would come out first............ It fucking sucks that the index, from yeaaaaars ago, is still the leading PCVR headset. This has GOT to change soon. That, or they need to drop it's price from $1000, because it's getting straight up out-classed at this point by $400 headsets that are even MOBILE.

I just want PCVR....... is that so much to ask for?

text:
The Quest 3 is VERY good and it's much better than the Vision Pro. Stats might be a little better on the Vision Pro and the "see through" might be in a better quality as well.
But I still prefer the Quest 3, even if the prices were the same, which they are certainly not!
Try playing Half Life Alex on this thing. It's amazing! You don't need wires. I thought it would suck to stream games from your pc without a wire, but it's not.
You don't even need your laptop that you are streaming from, to be connected using a wire if you use the Vertical Desktop app instead of Steam Link.
You can also try Asgards Wrath 2. It runs directly from the device and it's a great experience as well.
My girl friend and I are having blast playing Elven Assassin together. We each have a Quest 3 and the game is soo good. It's a game where you shoot arrows. I thought it might suck on these devices, I thought it would be inaccurate, but it's certainly not!
I can also work on this thing using a bluetooth keyboard and mouse, works like a charm. I also don't get seasick while wearing it for a long time. I did with the first Oculus Rift dev edition I got in 2014.
Regarding battery life. There are so many great options for that, so don't worry.

text: I returned my quest 2 very quickly when I bought it, I didnt like the controllers or the image quality. I was always getting vertigo anytime a menu would open in VR and I spent more time feeling sick and wanting to yank it off my head than I did playing anything, poker worked great and beat saber but everything else was just not usable for me. I got the quest 3 about 4 days ago and I almost instantly forgot that I have a gaming PC, the image quality is SPECTACULAR and I FINALLY dont get sick at fast movements. I can fire up population one and play literally until the battery dies and then still be mad that I had to stop playing. Ive never felt so immersed into games before, the hand tracking is more of a gimmick right now but those controllers are awesome. I did this really cool test in population one, they have a training area with every weapon in the game and a huge firing range. Now in most games im a self professed god tier sniper with a mouse and keyboard but in games like Apex I tend to miss shots a LOT. I grabbed this big sniper rifle in VR and got to load the mag and rack the slide and man did that ever make me happy. You get this awesome haptic feedback from the controllers and hear that bolt rack into place and then put your other hand out to cradle the rifle and then just settle right into the scope. With ZERO practice I walked up next to this guy that was missing every single shot with his single shot sniper rifle and just sighted down the scope and started nailing the center of every single target. I had to really focus down the sights and work to control my breathing and my small twitches because its so sensitive to every little movement. You have to find the target, sight down the scope, control your breathing, steady your arms, and squeeze the trigger just like a real gun. Ive pulled off some insane mile shots where I had to thread a gap and the amount of control I had to have to keep that sight right where I needed it was insanity. You can put your arm out and turn the gun every which way, I can pick up an smg and move while shooting at someone 90 degrees to the right of me, I can peek around corners, I can crouch and hide, I can turn the gun sideways and play gangsta if I want, the headset and controllers just know what you want to do and its so immersive. I get a little sick because im utterly terrified of heights when I climb but the second im up and standing it passes. Now when im climbing way up I have a tendency to just pause and look to the sides of me to just enjoy the view. I mean can most of us even remember the last time we played a game and just stopped to enjoy the scenery? Assassins creed was probably the last time I did that, the one where you play a Spartan, this thing constantly has me stopping to just say wow and then im right back into playing the soldier calling out as im clearing rooms with my squad. Then im over playing at the VR tables and talking to random people, or im off to play Bait and go fishing at a lake somewhere, or maybe I go into Meta Horizons and visit new worlds that have tons of interactive activities. I can fish and ride a boat around in one world, fly paper airplanes in another, its just endless. Theres so much stuff to do and then they have a subscription that gives you 2 free games every month that your subscribed and the games are like $30 each. I will not be returning this headset at all, its more fun than my PC and believe me im the most anti console person you could probably find, I hate being forced to upgrade to get access to new releases but VR is just so FUN. Its one thing to be involved in the story or run around in Apex but its another monster entirely when the gun is in your hands and you can hear the enemies footsteps around you on different kinds of materials, poke your head out and get blasted by a sniper hiding in a tower. Its dynamic and so real, its like a neverending game of laser tag or airsoft that you dont have to wait months to do and pay hundreds of dollars.

text: Having an Index, a Reverb G2 with it's own extra set of lighthouses and knuckles, and now the Quest 3, I can rather confidently give the nod to the Quest 3 so far. The ability  to use the Quest 3 standalone, and even more importantly wireless to my PC has made it firmly into my daily driver. Just losing the need to have my position constantly in the back of my mind to track the wire even with a full pulley system, and the associated light tugs on my head as I moved, has been such a massive increase in immersion that exceeded expectations massively. Passthrough is also far more convenient and useful than I even expected. More time will tell, but I am fully endorsing Quest 3 at this time.

text:
🎯 Key Takeaways for quick navigation:

00:01 📈 O úspěchu Quest 2 a otázce vývoje Quest 3
- Meta prodala téměř 20 milionů headsetů Quest 2.
- Úspěch Quest 2, ale ztráta Meta ve výši 47 miliard dolarů, což ukazuje na investice do výzkumu a vývoje.
01:22 🎮 První dojmy z používání Quest 3
- Nový sensorový systém umožňuje realistický video průhled a rychlou interakci s okolím.
- Hodnocení výkonu a vlastností headsetu včetně displeje, výkonu a baterie.
03:42 📺 Kvalita displeje a výkon
- Dual 2064x2208 displeje s vysokým rozlišením a širokým zorným polem.
- Nekvalita displejů ve srovnání s OLED displeji a absenci očního sledování.
04:38 🔥 Výkon a tepelná správa
- Vysoký výkon a grafika s XR2 Gen 2 čipem.
- Problémy s tepelným vývojem a zhoršenou výdrží baterie.
06:04 🎮 Kontrolery a sledování
- Vylepšený design ovladačů, ale některé problémy se sledováním.
- Možnost použití sledování rukou, i když s omezenou spolehlivostí.
07:57 🌐 Rozšířená realita a pracovní aplikace
- Zkušenosti s rozšířenou realitou a pracovními aplikacemi.
- Potenciál pro budoucí vývoj a zlepšení.
11:54 💰 Cena a výběr headsetu
- Porovnání Quest 3 s Quest 2 a doporučení pro různé typy uživatelů.
- Zvážení alternativ a budoucnost standalone VR headsetů.
13:47 💼 Reklama a závěrečné myšlenky
- Reklama na kabely s technologií StealthSense.
- Shrnutí a závěrečné myšlenky o Quest 3 a jeho možné konkurenci.

text: I've owned every Oculus headset since DK1, and I was really unimpressed with the specs for the Quest 3 and the preview videos which barely look better than the Quest 2. I also didn't give 2 cents about the dumb AR stuff. But, I went ahead and bought a Quest 3 figuring I'd just play with it for a week and return it when I was unimpressed. Well - so much for that idea. The AR stuff, and color pass through, was so much better than I was expecting, that it pretty much instantly became the reason for keeping it. Also, none of the games I had were updated for the Quest 3, so as far as graphics go, I wasn't really impressed - then I went ahead and bought Red Matter 2 - an wow, what an upgrade, that was the first (and so far only) game I've seen that made me take notice and really be impressed and think this is, in fact, a next-gen unit. I think Meta is facing the same issue you always have with VR, and that is how you advertise it to people in 2D, and I'd say they've done an exceptionally poor job of advertising the Quest 3. In person, it's a much better upgrade over the Quest 2 than I thought it would be. Although I'll disagree with Linus on the awful headscrap - throw that thing away and get a 3rd party battery strap, they're so much more comfortable and basically double your run time, for like $50.

text:
Just upgraded to the Quest 3 last night from my CV-1 (cable is going out).
The resolution jump is incredible; the image quality not so much. I have terrible mura even in dim game enviroments, artifacting even in standalone and glitches distortions etc in steam link. Controllers lose tracking and shoulder reaches fail 50% of the time. Quality control is just not good here. Sound distorts and warbles both streaming and on link, wired or wireless. Seriously, even right after a reboot the basic interface gliches and hiccups. Set is waaaaay hotter than my CV1, battery lasts about an hour, even with brightness turned down. AR is very grainy and the warping is everywhere, its only really effective at walking around in lomovision land.
I am not sure why all the big tech channels like LTT are gushing over these things without acknowleging all the user complaints about mura variance between different units, battery issues and the doom that is trying to get repairs from Meta, who will literally tell you that your replacement hardware will be sourced from other defective returns not refurbished and that they are "working on an updates". Some of this may be early adopter issues but I have never been so initially stoked and then immediately frustrated by these quality issues.
Also, do not buy this from Gamestop; according to the call center I will be lucky to get store credit tomorrow, the manager may still refuse a defective return and force me to exchange it through Meta which just means getting a different defective unit in exchange, hoping for the flaws in the next one to be lesser. I really was hoping that this was it since just about every other headset uses a propritary connector based on oculink and I am not going through the CV1 paperweight experience again.
Wish me luck!

text:
I've had a Lenovo WMR headset for around 6 years now. VR blew my mind but felt like a gimmick so far, with games I would play for 15 mintues at a time, maybe more with Beat Saber and such, but that's it.

I got the Quest 3 a week ago and it's blowing my mind still today. The clarity of the passthrough is pretty good, but just the fact of being able to see the room in real time is a game changer. I can drink, I can talk to my wife, I can avoid the dog, reposition myself... It feels like a perfect short-break tool.

I am also surprised at how well apps run natively in the headset, and PCVR is pretty good too. Of course there's space for improvements, for me mostly battery life (i'm playing longer sessions than ever!) and heat dissipation, but this feels like a very polished product.

I totally recommend it for people who want to start on VR but also for those who are a bit tired of wired options.

text:
Im still waiting for "the next big thing" when it comes to VR. It seems like there are tons of VR headsets out there that are all tackling 1 of the many problems I have with VR.

Some headsets give you really wide FOVs, some do their best to make the image as clear and not blurry as possible, some are very small and light weight, some have really good external cameras/ AR capabilities, etc etc. But very few are trying to make a headset that tries to fix all those problems at the same time. And for me even when one of those issues is present it completely ruins my experience and turns it from a "wow I could do this for hours every day!" to a gimmick thats fun for maybe an hour or 2 a week.

I adore flight simulation and cars. Once the hardware gets to a solid 8k w/120-140 FOV I will fully dive into VR. But for now....my Quest 2 mostly sits in a drawer because its still so much easier to just play a good 2d game after that 2-3 month VR high.

text:
Haven't tried the Quest 3, but from all the reviews I have seen I think that this product also made me more hyped about Apple Vision Pro. With it I could basically have an infinite number of high res monitors all around me. Probably with high color accuracy since Apple is making it. I also assume that Apple will bring some enhanced version of Sidecar so that you can use your more powerful Mac Studio to run heavy programs, while only using the Vision Pro as an Extended Reality display. (Maybe just wirelessly transferring App windows, rather the whole OS, to minimize percieved latency when turning your head?)

My biggest worry about Apple Vision Pro (and similar headsets) is comfort. Is it comfortable to do an entire work-day with these on? What about watching a 3 hour movie? Is it comfortable wearing a good headset at the same time?
I'm less worried about battery since there is the possibility for swapping batteries with the Vision Pro, and there will probably be a market for third party power accessories with higher battery capacity.


Maybe the VR revolution is less about games and 3D work, and actually just about having a portable high quality screen?
At work today I had to use Premiere Pro on a 14" MacBook Pro without external displays. A horrible experience as I end up spending more time rearranging my workspace and finding the correct panel, rather than actually editing. A high quality Extended Reality headset connected to the same laptop would have been a fantastic experience since I could have seen more panels at the same time.
Maybe we'll buy laptops without displays in the future, just connecting a VR/XR headset instead? (NUCs with batteries?)

The price needs to come down, of course. But I could imagine a lot of users being interested in replacing monitors if the quality and comfort is right.
author: @motionblurgamer

text:
@@test-zg4hv If you haven’t already sold your soul like I have, I assume there will be good alternatives from other companies in about two years. If pixel density and color accuracy isn’t that important, I guess even a Quest 3 could be enough for a lot of people. Assuming of course that software integration is good enough.

I do hope though that there will be apps that make Windows PCs able to connect to Apple Vision Pro. While I do my creative work on Mac, I still play games on PC.
Even if Apple continues to improve Mac gaming at its current phase, I still think it will be a few years before I’m ready to make the PC my secondary gaming device.

author: @MTNorville

text: That's sorta how the Lighthouse system works on competing VR headsets. You put up base stations which shoot out lasers in a sweeping arc, which is how the headset and controllers track their positions. They're excellent for accuracy and can track the controllers even when they're not in front of the headset, but they're expensive, require additional setup and have occlusion issues if the player's body blocks them.

text:
Funny how the is gushing over it. But really its a massive joke that has no practical use. Hell it cannot even work for gaming as VR headsets violate a very key thing. This is the reason why you don't actual move forward in a normal walking motion. Why games teleport you everywhere you need to go instead of just pressing a stick to move forward.

IF your body is standing still but your eyes depict yourself as moving. This causes your brain to malfunction and gives you imminence sense of nausea. As the reality of this is VR is a gimmick. Everything about it from Shopping in VR to even playing video games. Has been already bee done better with just typing into a search engine.
author: @rman88

text: Almost every VR game has an option to walk forward with pushing the stick. VR sickness is usually overcome within a couple weeks of playing and getting used to it. It's commonly referred to as "getting your VR legs". Most of the VR community dislike teleportation movement, so most games now don't even use teleportation as the default. That was back in the vive days mostly. We've come a long way since then.

text:
I'm using it almost exclusively for PC VR with Virtual Desktop and the upgrade from Quest 2 is crazy good for me. One of the first things I noticed about the Quest 2 when I first got it was how tiny the sweet spot is and how basically moving your eyes naturally is not an option if you actually want to see clearly what you are looking at, so I ended up acting like a neck-less robot in VR. And the moment I played any game that required quicker movements, just one millimeter sliding of the headset on my face would be enough to move that sweet spot away from the center of my vision. The only solution to that was to crank the head strap violently to my face so the headseat would not move and that was incredibly uncomfortable even with the fanciest 3rd party straps and facial interfaces.
All of that is solved with the Q3. The image clarity is way better and the sweet spot is huge. Plus the IPD granular adjustment is actually useful. I don't even own one of the 3rd party straps yet, but with a battery strapped to the back with some velcro it's already decently balanced and I don't have to have it so tight that my face falls off. That in itself was enough reason to upgrade, but then there's the more compact build, the nicer controllers, the usable pass-through, better speakers - it ended up being a no-brainer upgrade for me.

text:
This headset is simply unparalleled, offering an extraordinary experience at an incredibly competitive price. A few years ago, Microsoft attempted to usher us into the ‘future’ with Hololens, but the technology was still lagging behind the vision. What’s truly astounding about Zuckerberg’s presentation is how the Quest 3 utilizes your real-world environment to generate 3D images and assets, providing an immersive experience right within your headset.

However, I must emphasize that after investing billions in R&D, Meta should now consider allocating resources to their gaming departments. The Quest 3, despite its impressive technology, still lacks a compelling library of titles to convince potential buyers. It’s high time to match the device’s technological prowess with a diverse range of engaging games.

You can buy much better VR experience for such price.

text:
There's a few errors in this video:
2:40 The headset specifically features a LiDAR sensor, not a Time-of-Flight sensor. This may seem pedantic, but there really aren't any consumer products in 2023 that use the term "Time-of-Flight" instead of "LiDAR".
2:45 IPD has nothing to do with comfort. The IPD adjustment wheel is to adjust the IPD (duh) to ensure that everything is in focus and also so that virtual objects appear to be the correct size.
2:54 There is no evidence to support that the 3.5mm jack is a combo jack. There is no support for external microphones when using the Quest standalone.

author: @daylen577

text: Especially as lighthouses are still inside out tracking, so it should be working on Standalone VR devices without any problems anyway. So even if Valve is going the standalone VR route too (as it simply is the biggest leap out of the niche that VR was/still is), they could still offer better tracking with lighthouses (maybe even optional, so they can still compete with Quests price without the lighthouses). Also, I feel like they wouldn't go USB-C only but support e. g. DisplayPort or Mini-DisplayPort, as they definitely have more stakes in PCVR than Meta does. My hopes is still that they'll offer two VR headsets. One standalone to compete with Quest in quality and price, and a PCVR only one to skip out on internal processing units, maybe add eye tracking based rendering (which makes more sense for PS5/PC rendering than standalone rendering, as the payoff is way higher and energy consumption is less important), so they can offer significantly better specs with only a slightly increased the price.

text:
Lots of people love metaverses. Rec Room is HUGE. VRChat is also really really big, and people even play those without VR headsets, which is a FAR inferior experience of course.

Dystopia is locked in with how we didn't address climate change. Young people know this, older people still think there's hope cause their heads are still firmly buried in the sand.

Once you accept a dystopian future, VR becomes a lot more appealing. The world has steadily gotten worse for the last 35 or so years. Gaming has gotten better and better. People already just slump down in front of a screen for hours and hours and hours. VR is just a screen on your face, forcing you to move around and be active. If you want a real dystopian image, just look at the folks who spend 5 hours playing CS every damn night with their friends. 30 years ago, those people would be out in the world hanging out with people. That time has come and gone. Most of the people who watch this channel have been living that dystopian lifestyle in 60hz on a flat screen for a couple of decades....

Bet you didn't even have the time to think about how much of a dystopia your grandpa sees that as? Too much fun playing video games to even care about the fact that many of us wasted literally thousands of hours playing video games.. Loving every second. THAT is dystopian to some.

The world is getting worse, VR is getting better.
CO2 stays in the air for 300 years. We've sealed our fate.
Get into VR now or stay seated in front of your monitors pretending we're not hurtling towards the endtimes, that's your choice.
Me, I'll be playing VR while the rest of you burn the world down around me.

text:
To those who own the Quest 3 including those who've been through many different headsets over the VR timeline. How is it?

TL;DR - Tried a few VR HMDs, gave up on them years ago and kept one. Should I jump back on board with the Meta Q3?


A couple of years ago I bought a Reverb G2 and a Index to give them both a try and see which one I preferred more. I had already dipped my toes in with a Quest 1 since it was affordable and reasonably competent; I wasn't convinced by the tech until there had been a couple of iterations of HMDs.
Reverb had amazing fidelity but the FOV was mediocre and really hurt it more than I thought relative to the Index. Controllers on G2 felt like fisher price dogshit and I actively disliked using them. Not to mention WMR was... well just awful.
Index: Fantastic product, just a bit too bulky and it's a shame the screen tech wasn't higher fidelity yet. The controllers were GOATed though.

I sold them both in the end, I'd have liked to have kept the index or at least the controllers but I couldn't justify the price given how much use I thought I'd get out of them.
I kept the Quest 1 because it's portable and fun to use for some lightweight stuff.

This Quest 3 looks pretty solid. Love this passthrough, usable hand tracking and the big spec bump and HMD design improvements plus it's a good price. The controllers, I can't say but the OG Quest controllers were pretty comfy. I'd say they were great if only the Index hadn't achieved perfection.

text:
I agree that the improvements are nice, but it's getting aggravating how little effort they seemingly put into ergonomics and comfort. While the default strap feels reasonably okay for a 10 minutes try, after an hour it starts to feel like a literal torture device, causing stabbing headaches and rubbing out the skin on my cheeks - the foam padding should be at least twice as soft IMO. And for me personally the facial interface in general was a big disappointment, because I think my FOV is totally not good with the Quest 3 - the lenses are very far from my eyes even with the smallest setting. The accessories that would make it borderline wearable are insanely overpriced. I think Meta needs to start paying attention to the factors they've been largely ignoring, and provide an actually comfortable headset with a proper strap and multiple facial interfaces to make sure everybody can have a good experience. Not to mention how shit the software is... You install a demo that you and up hating, and you cannot remove it from your library, no filters for MR content or games that have been updated for Q3, plus you have to use little boxes to play videos and read text in the store, like if you used a monitor; absolutely no proper customization for VR. And even the best games (like Red Matter 2) are still incredibly superficial compared to PC games. So, overall I appreciate the advancements, but I'd say that the package is still not there, and I personally found myself totally not using the unit after the first 2-3 weeks. I hate to say this, because I've never in my life bought an Apple product, but I start to think that we need Apple to show them how to hell to develop an actually good product that has comfort and retention.

Btw, the battery is the least of the problems for me - I can attach a 20K battery bank and put it into my pocket. I actually wish they stopped shoving a heavy brick on my face and moved the battery elsewhere.

text: I own The Q3 and aome of the arguments in this review are valid but others are a little ... meh. First off Virtual Desktop is MUST for PCVR. Im sure they didnt include VD because it was not from Meta and evaluating it without addon software that cost extra $$ is what they were after. But it is the go to app for all PCVR and is worth mentioning as an option as it basically addresss all the complaints LTT had with airlink. The hand tracking is also something to improve but I did notice that they way they were grabbing things was a little off. If you Grab windows with a full fist it seems to work well. That being said LED lights at night will cause hand tracking to skitz out. Natural daylight is best for that.  I think the biggest thing never seems to be mentioned that really bugs me is that traditional PCVR need base stations SETUP in a specific room !! I can casually play PCVR in my living room , out on my lawn, in the kitchen hell even in the effing bathroom. Ive even used Virtual Dekstop to play PCVR in my office at work using my home PC as a game streaming server.  Linus and co seem to forget that the place where you have your PC is not always the best place to play VR. In my case my office is just too small to play proper VR so being in my living room able to play HL Alyx is ideal.  I think this review is a little disingenuous as many PCVR enthusiasts including myself love the Q2 and now Q3 and i think this review only shows it from a PCVR snob perspecive

1. they claimed battery life an issue but didnt address its not a big deal when you get a headstrap with built in battery

3. they didnt bother to check virtual desktop experiece for PCVR, Q3 only headset to support AV1 which not only I was able to play games identical to cable experience but I managed to stream my PCVR games through 5G network which is crazy, Virtual desktop supports AV1.
4. the hot part is only felt at the outside but you never notice this heat from inside.
5. recommending base stations in 2023 is actually dumb, its 500$ + controller cost + 1000$ headsets cost yet they complain about 500$ price tag of Q3
author: @TRX25EX

text:
@TRX25EX  I understand everything you said, but it was mainly an out of the box experience review and a fair one imo. Additional peripherals, software, and RTX cards add up, so I figured they were actively avoiding talking about those improvements to just show case the device mostly as is. My biggest gripe was the hand tracking. It isn't perfect, but it wasn't nearly as bad as what they showed. idk, maybe Canadians have weird hand shapes. I have no argument on base station suggestions. That's not where the market seems to be heading anymore, and I started on a rift S so I skipped all of that anyway. I'll assume they were suggesting them to people who are tracking snobs with a lot of money.
Final thoughts on this review are this: they are just tech youtubers and not VR enthusiast youtubers. They've done an alright job showing off the quest 3 and they have a ton of subscribers, so more sold headsets for those that may be interested in this tech, which isn't a bad thing in my book

All in all, coming from somebody who really dislikes facebook / meta, im sure glad someone is pushing the tech. Without somebody doing it, VR would die out or stay a permanent niche. And even considering how (comparably) mainstream it became, it still struggles with player retention. A lot. author: @RWDtech

text: "how have they lost so much money and manage to still have the most successful headset". The answer is more complicated. They have made NOTHING off the Reality Labs business unit... When Microsoft launched XBox it took them five years and two iterations to begin to turn a profit. They funded XBox with money from other divisions... Meta followed this model with Oculus, buying Oculus but continuing to loose money year after year... Next year will be 10 years since Meta purchased Oculus and the span between their losses/spending and earnings are still around -$8 billion (yep, BILLION) a year... Unlike Microsoft, which started flat but by the third year had profits going up and spending leveling off, Meta has seen a decline in interest with sales dropping and spending increasing.... No other company in the history of tech has ever taken the kind of losses and bet and big as Meta has bet on the long term vision of XR... There is no successful business model behind any of this. Not yet and not likely for years to come. It's amazing.... A think a lot of people inside Reality Labs who understand business have seen the writing on the wall... Zuckerberg is kind of like a madman with a heart defibrillator on his VR investment corpse, he has enough money to keep the illusion of a successful product line going for some time. But I think Carmack, et al left in large part because there's nothing sustainable and the public has spoken, there just isn't enough interest to get to profitability... They would have to sell about six times as many headsets to begin generating enough revenue from the app store commissions to fund the R&D expenses and to get to a product 6x the number of customers will actually buy, the R&D expenses aren't going down... Apple may can move those kind of numbers for a general compute HMD, but Meta's market is gaming. It's not going to happen for them.... But Apple isn't planning to make this kind of "break-even-on-hardware" mistake. The Vision Pro's build price supposedly around $1500. So Apple has their typical hardware margins baked into the retail price. Apple's not going to live in fantasy land. The device will be making money day one. I bet by the 2nd or 3rd iteration Vision Pro will get to mass market pricing. Meta has set pricing expectation on break-even hardware trying to copy the game console model that's going to burn them badly if Vision Pro takes off. Meta's days in this space are probably numbered.

Either way I'll be holding off until the next steam system unless facebook or apple make a convincing foray and start getting more titles with 'depth'. text:
Honestly, the issue with VR is still the software.
Gaming needs push the technology to the point where consumers will want to engage with it. But there aren't games on VR. There are "VR-games", but when you think VR-games, you think low-budget spin-off or indie project. Only rare cases push the technology to its max. Actual games need to be there for it.

Only then will VR become something you can engage with on a proper level. We always hear talk of a Metaverse. But that has already existed for ages in MMOs, just not in VR.
MMOs are multi-multi-multi-million dollar projects. That creates a "metaverse" because the content is engaging and people are in the world there to interact with.
Throwing in a voice chat with VR avatars will be attractive to some, but end-all it is just a voice chat with some interactive elements.

text:
I always used outside tracking before and now on Quest 3, I have a huge problem in Beat Saber - everything looks OK, and the sabers are tracked correctly - I do not have issues like the sabers flying through the air to places where they are not or completely disappearing... but on Hard+ difficulties in fast-paced songs, some blocks are missed even though I feel that I should have gotten them.

I know Linus mentioned that twice in the review, but to me it is a way bigger issue than it seems in the review - I am wondering if I am just plain bad, if it is a technical limitation of the inside-out tracking, or if I even have a faulty unit. #feelsbadman

Anyone else with similar experience?
author: @cezeta

text: Probably an issue for all inside-out tracking headsets. Same happens on the Pico 4. Haven't had the issue with the Index.
author: @Dragonissa

text: I still have a Rift S from 2019 which also features inside-out tracking. I mostly play beat saber on Expert/Expert+ and normally don't really have any big issues which really blows my mind considering that everything is tracked by the headset itself and its also 4,5 years old already. I did notice some occasional tracking issues when blocks are either on the far left or the far right of my position. But I actually noticed that I was too slow in hitting them so when I adapted to that and hit the blocks a bit earlier + using a full swing and not when they were already nearly behind me it worked much better and I can play my songs without any real tracking issues which again kind of amazes me.

text:
I tried it and my thoughts are no, not over hyped, good for a lot of content, movies, games. You can travel with this, even in a car it will be good. There's down sides, wearing the headset for a movie is annoying but the movie looks really good, like a lot better than expected. The 3D 180 content is next level realism, feel like you are really walking the streets of Germany or France, Egypt. I see it as a game changer in the virtual reality takeover. Phones should have this, soon.

No offense, but I don't think a man your age knows or understands all what this device really is, since you grew up in a digital internet space. It's impossible for you to understand the progression, the least talked about feature for me, is the movie screen and Youtube apps. The movie theater screen appears to be 4k, this is a big deal. Why do I need a TV anymore ?. I was playing Mortal combat 11, runs perfectly, with the X-Box edition. You guys see this as some neat feature, a 4k movie screen is more than a neat feature. Watching a old movie, looks better than ever before, hands down. My eyes are 63mm apart and Quest has a 63mm preset, in the middle, so they are made for me. It has a 68mm and a 58mm setting.

Again, I see 3D content as a next level of realism, in 8k it's stupidly 3D. I see the headsets as being backwards compatible with image quality, I barely expected a true 4k experience in 2D and I'd say it is close to 4k. The desktop and browser is somewhat workable, it works better than on my laptop, depending on wifi I am shocked at the responsiveness of the UI. To say theater mode is awesome, is very true. I can take this in my bag and use the wifi wherever I go, and chill and play games, watch movies, podcasts, Youtube, social media and again it beats my laptop for that, by a mile and what else does it need to do ?.

Watching a movie on my laptop works badly, so does a game...this is like having a X-Box with a built in TV, the size of a theater room, I can play Mortal Kombat. The pancake lenses I see as whatever, the X-BOX edition works in the dark and I use it in the dark, I think it's better for gaming. The head strap and thing makes me sweat a lot, it is heavy for a movie. I suggest a bandanna. Dude, I am impressed as a tech junkie and addict, 20% visual quality might be worth it, but if you're looking for the best deal, X-box edition. I think the 3S visual quality is good, 4k is good, I see 8K content looks upscaled, so it looks good anyways. I don't have any issue with the lenses being too thick, if anything my eyes are more blind than anything else. I find when using glasses, it's even cleaner.

If you're holding out for something then I would wait for a phone with 8K cam and VR straps, those will be around and the glasses, VR glasses with sim card and I would like cameras since, why not ?. I see VR glasses replacing the phone, later on as the tech gets smaller, first set will be like goggles, large frame glasses. I think yeah, it makes being out in public different, have to be responsible, if everyone has a 8K VR camera on their heads, only a matter of time before the camera and sensors fit a pair of glasses, then that's what people will want to use.

Soon, all the action cams will record VR or they already do. My prediction is no, eventually they won't even cause cancer, but phones are too much radiation, any way you think about it, people will be wearing a powered device on their heads, where their eyes are going to be. It uses far less electricity, my headset works with 5g wifi or 5GHZ, people are all worried that will destroy the world, 5G. You guys will have to get used to how all this technology works, cell phones came out in 2009 and people still are not used to these.

You guys literally have no clue what is going on, right now, but everything is about to transfer over to VR, cause it's that big of a deal, not a gimmick for using 3D apps but it will be the new standard for graphics, some day in the near future we will ditch our PC's and phones, just have these glasses for most things. I will still need a PC for apps, but you guys use too many apps for this machine, now. People won't have the option for a TV, a console, a PC, it's too much plus a VR headset and a phone, this will all become one unit and will work in conjunction with audio and graphic boxes, USB hubs. Right now, people don't even understand that audio cards have been replaced by a audio interface and if it's bluetooth, that means it can link with your Quest and that means all audio can be recorded and shaped, in Abelton or Pro Tools. We can get GPU's that use a linked graphics and provide ram and processor, in the future. Right now, I would like a new audio interface with bluetooth in and out.

Next, I am working on a speaker array, studio monitors are dumb, I want to make this multi use speaker that can actually handle multimedia, I would use a large box, though, 15 inch woofer and then more speakers, make my own subwoofer and PA speaker, for when I need to play bass, any instrument through the monitor speakers and that will work. I see a 8 inch sub as barely enough to hear bass, a 15 is needed and it's a lot, I mean a lot of money to buy a subwoofer, that big. I can build a speaker and this would be a lot more economical, get a bluetooth amp, make a cabinet, buy speakers, some JBL's or something. I can place the speaker like 15 feet behind me, and that would sound good, perfect location in the studio, yeah you need one of those.

That's if you can even tell me what a studio is anymore, most people no, they just do games, a studio is a digital space now, a PC and a keyboard, or three of those, a Mic system, hubs. Can you guys build one, I doubt it cause it was hard for me, a professional to make a good workspace, that is effective. People have nice rigs, but they are nice in a weird way, mine is only nice cause it has functional elements, my setup is better. My setup is optimized for the future and it looks more industrial like a bunker, the walls are full of screens, speakers and whatever, lights. If everything is set up right, I want nothing on my desk besides essentials and I wall mount my screens, lights. For my mic, use a pole mount and not the other one.

You need hubs, like 3-4. My pole has a USB-C hub, hi speed at the bottom, so you plug in a camera it has a 3 foot power cord, the USB is right there. Then it has reader and extra hubs, on the left side, I never have to bend down into the PC, everything is tucked away. All the acces is above, on the desk. If I need extra keyboard, no problem I have two and a trackball, and a 48 key musical keyboard /controller, always plugged into 3 USB plugs. This means a lot of people can use my PC, one side is for music, movies, the other is for everything else, games, work, editing.

I had to buy a lot of cables, like 300$ worth of those, hubs, that's not including the power strips, cable management trays or the other things, ducts, the fast charger. I spent a lot, on just power delivery, like 800$. Now that everything is tucked away and operational, nothing could be sweeter. Say, a guitarist a DJ with a turntable and a bassist come over, I can record them all, at the same time, while streaming it, they can sing and that's ok, I have a mic. My other option, use 2 PC's and then I would lose all the integration into one PC. I had to use the integrated GPU, to run my TV. I used to have all my wires in a bucket, horrible. Right now, I have 5 open USB and 4 110 outlets, a UBC that is open, this means I have enough open, for any reason I needed to plug in 5 devices. A phone, a webcam, a video camera, a light, extra mic, I can do that just on one pole, It's like a 4 foot pole, I could use more USB -C. If I wanted to get fancy, get a 4 foot RGB tube and mount it to the pole, with the zip ties, anything. I would still be able to mount a camera, more on the back side, even with the tube on there, I guess a gopro 12's would be the best, for a webcam or anything else, use the goosenecks. That would look good for recording, a big light.

If you guys are not impressed, then you must be highly confused, spoiled and not sure how to use the device. It's definitely worth 3-500$. I see it as a highly capable device. The Bluetooth works, controller works, everything simply works, the thing watching your hands gets annoying, if you smoke or do literally anything with your hands, besides sit on them, a snap gesture is needed.
Sometimes when watching a video, the UI appears too low, straining my neck to look down. It's a highly teched out gadget.

text: I only have Meta 2. Understand the premise of M3 though, with the pass thru boost and mixed reality capacity. My feeling is that the Metaverse is Soooooooooooooooooo close to being an epic innovation event that'll put multiple entire Fortune 500 companies out of business. Yet it's not there yet. How can Mr. Zuckerberg become the absolute undisputed tech King of Kings? He has to improve the Battery Life + field of View + Resolution + content Library + Ergonomics / lightness of these headsets. With Meta 2 my initial experience was gasping in utter awe at the 3d immersion experience...but then like gee this is a bit heavy, clunky, annoying, on my head. Then a lot of the content on the meta store were gimmicky games or stunt like stuff. A skyscraper plank walk. A roller coaster ride while a skeleton is sitting next to you and you are being chased by a dinosaur. Virtual mini golf or table tennis. Etc. On and on like this. Compare these to robust games and apps like Fallout 4 or Call of the Sea, or Microsoft Excel or Adobe Premiere. History repeats. When cinema just started out, you had gimmicky stunts like footage of a train about to hit the audience. The initial 'nickelodeon' theaters had no George Lucas or Steven Spielberg type master storytellers yet. Audience's ate it up for a while. Metaverse MIGHT make all the paradigms for phones, TVs, and laptops we currently have now and inherited from the 20th century, feel completely stale. Like how personal computers once made IBM mainframes seem. Are companies with a heavy emphasis on big bulky awkward atom based TVs, mouses, or keyboards dead men walking? I.e. Sony, Samsung, Logitech, Vizio, Toshiba, LG, Onn, TCL. Why the hell, once these headsets are perfected, would you want to spend even a single penny on a BIG HEAVY 20th century paradigm TV, that requires a big table / stand / arm system to hold up? That limit your mobility? That takes up a lot of space in rooms? That can't follow you around to another room. Say from the living room to kitchen to patio to bathroom to bedroom. That is hard to adjust if you just change your body position and thus viewing angle. Say go from sitting to standing to lying down flat in your body position? For $200 bucks with even just the Meta 2 you can feel like you are in the front room at a movie theater with a huge wall sized TV, that can be moved around smooth as silk to exactly the spot you desire for perfect viewing. You can be sitting on the metro waiting for your train to arrive with a giant 80 inch virtual TV in front of you and with total visual immersion / privacy. Then when the train arrives, walk onto the train and sit down and reposition the "TV" in exactly the right spot visually and continue watching your content. These types of scenarios are an absolute game changer. Once people no longer buy 20th century type TVs in ten years. This will affect CONTENT companies like Disney, HBO, or Paramount massively too. They have such a psychological and substantial investment in 20th century paradigms for content creation and distribution. Can they cross the bridge into the 21st century? For some reason IBM and Xerox could not become Microsoft or Apple. History now likely repeats. "NEW wine in NEW wine skins".

text:
THE STRAP - IS NOT BAD. DONT BE SUCKED INTO BUYING ADD-ONS!

In seriousness haha tho it's going to be very dependent on each person's which is proven as reviews on comfort are polarized.

I've logged 10 hours over 3 prolonged sessions SO FAR.. I'm bald and have a medium-sized head. I have found it well balanced and comfortable without being hot (shocking more pads and coverage add more heat right!)

My wife on the other hand. 10 minutes complained of pain on her neck and forehead. I said waaaa no way, took it off her resized her from scratch and 10 minutes later she said the same. We assumed for her it's just her head being smaller and narrower shaped than mine and you know I'm a male gamer and she's a real female 👀 so it would probably take some getting used counterbalance neck strength etc etc... Chill don't cancel ❌.

Comfort 8\10 for me (what do u expect stock there is always upsells)
Comfort 4\10 for my wife. She says a thicker more padded strap would be her style

text:
I have to admit that I prefer Quest 2 over 3. I don't know why, but my quest 3 seems slightly grainier. As if whatever they did to create the pass-through leaves a sort grainy appearance to blue sky, etc. You can't see it on the Quest 3 environment, but it's there in all my games. It's like looking through a window screen. Yeah, you can see quite a bit without noticing it, but it's there. No reviewer has complained about it. But it drives me nuts. As does the feeling of blinders on the sides of my vision. I honestly feel the Q2 view is better, clearer and more open.

Also, for whatever reason, the Q3 lens gets foggy with casual play. I have to wear a mask so I don't have to constantly wipe my lenses off. (With my safe to use, microfiber cloths.) As I use my Quest to work out, I worry this worsen when I am actually hot. I wear a scarf "rambo-style" to prevent sweat from getting on my headsets. This technique has worked with Q1 & Q2. I am uncertain if the scarf is adding to the lense steam or if it's because this new quest sits so much closer to the face.

I've tried pulling it out and away using the new adjusters, but the further away from my face, the worse the grainy view. Also, the more limited my range of view. A lot of reviewers say the field of view is bigger, but I honestly feel like it is WAY smaller.

I definitely love the new controllers.

As to the faster processing, I never had any trouble with with my Q2, so I assume I do not yet play any games that need one.

text:
@bartcoosemans6818 I am considering the same upgrade from rift s
I know for Q3 on paper (almost?) everything is better than Rift S, but could you give me some highlight for what made the biggest wow for you?
My current concerns are the Q3's headstrap, battery life (infinite on Rift S :D) and the PCVR over wifi (which RiftS doesn't have out of the box)
author: @rize2137

text: @@rize2137 Biggest wow is what you hear a lot... The image "crisp". The images are much better compared to the rift S. I got the elite strap so I cannot give my oppinion on the normal strap but the headset feels more comfortable to wear than the Rift S. Regarding the downsides, indeed as you mentioned, it is getting used to the battery life. Even when plugged in with the USB C into your PC (normal USB), it still drains the battery. I haven't tried a full gaming session to see how long it lasts, but coming from the Rift S, the battery is a downside. I have an external battery that I still have to try out on the quest but I need a longer usb C to usb C cable for that (and I woudln't be able to plug in both the battery and the pc since there is only one USB C). The second downside so far, is that MFS does'nt run as smooth it seems as mentioned due to the higher pixels, it takes more out of your PC (I got RTX 3080) however I'm currently using a cable to connect to the PC but the "VR desktop app" + using wifi 6 to connect wirelessly with your PC, would provide some tweaking oppertunities to get a better result. It would also mean I could charge up my Quest 3 with that external battery that I have... I have yet to test that out. This video was useful regarding MFS: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UVznwf0Z9h4&t=12s&ab_channel=VRFlightSimGuy. All in all, I would recommend the headset still. If you have a very good PC and want to play PC games most of the time, you could look into the more expensive VR headsets though. I chose the quest 3 because I didn't wanted to upgrade my PC just yet and I wanted wireless to watch videos/series. From now on I am more inclined to purchase games in the Oculus store whereas until now I tried to purchase everything in Steam. That way, I'll have more wireless options to play. Most of the time I'll be playing at home though so it doesn't matter for me personally but a lot of games will not work on quest 3 standalone if you purchased them on PC (like beat saber).

text:
My passthrough is pretty grainy. About on par with the Quest Pro, and looking at a phone was completely blown out and unusable. A factory reset helped the ISO a bit, but even still, it takes a long time to adapt, if it does at all.

A quick fix is looking at a bright light and then returning to the phone, or turning the phones brightness down completely. But it's regrettable that my experience is so much more of a hassle than what's been shared by creators with early access.

Beatsaber on PCVR is also basically unplayable through VD, as I'm not registering hits when it's too far out of my periphery.

All in all it's a great headset, but these things are pretty rough, and it's bordering on malicious that they put the headphone jack on the right hand side.

I'm a little bitter after all of this.

That stuff made me sick just thinking about wearing the headset. But I don’t think it helped that the headset was so top heavy and hot.

I can proudly say tho the quest 3 did not give me motion sickness nearly as bad as quest 2. The strap is ok but the headset itself is a huge improvement as far as comfort and I think that goes a long way in sickness experiences.

text: My tv has delay when using bluetooth headphones, but I fixed it by using an aptX bluetooth transmitter, then an aptX bluetooth receiver to plug the headphones into. Absolutely no lag. If the headphones already support aptX, then you just need the transmitter. The brand I bought was HomeSpot. Both transmitter and receiver need power though, so plug into a battery bank. My Anker 10000 has both usb-c for the headset, and a usb-a if needed for anything else.

text: 12:52 You're actually blocking an important IR tracking LED by using Quest 2 grips on the new Touch Plus controllers. 🤷 There's not only IR tracking LED's wrapped all the way around the controller's top face, but ALSO a single IR tracking LED on the inside edge of the bottom of the handle that helps significantly with calculating precise controller angle/orientation! That LED is being completely blocked with your current setup (if it hasn't been changed since).
author: @Cooe.

text: 5:50 Hard disagree. The new dual LCD panels' black levels/contrast and color saturation are NOTICABLY better than the Quest 2's single panel display! Sure, it's nowhere near the FALD backlit Quest Pro, let ALONE proper OLED levels but it IS a notable step in the right direction! 🤷
author: @Cooe.

text: 5:20 My experience here has actually been pretty different. While they almost entirely lack god-rays (light radiating out from bright objects), internal reflections/glare (meaning visible on the OPPOSITE side of the lens from the high-contrast object) are DEFINITELY still a major problem with Quest 3's pancake lenses! 🤷 It's not Bigscreen Beyond bad or anything like that, but it's definitely still an issue that needs solving with future hardware!
author: @Cooe.

text:
Sorry fans, but I'll be honest, I like to be honest.
I like "Meta Quest", I've been a user for years, I had "Meta Quest 2".
But I just bought the new "Meta Quest 3" for 556 euros and I was very disappointed.
I don't notice any difference in resolution, although they say it's 2k per eye. And I also notice the "screen door effect", they said it's barely noticeable, but that's a lie.
I know what I'm saying, because I still have "Meta Quest 2" and now I have the new "Meta Quest 3" to compare both.
I transferred the VR videos I had to the new "Meta Quest 3", with 6k each and good resolution, as soon as I watched them on "Meta Quest 3" I didn't see any improvement in resolution, and I used the same player "Deo VR ". Furthermore, you can also notice the "screen door effect", you can see the pixels, although it is slightly smaller.
I was very disappointed, if I had known I wouldn't have spent 556 euros.
It's better to wait a few years for "Meta Quest 4".
As for size, this headset is slightly thinner, but there is no less weight when we have it on our head, in fact there seems to be more weight on our forehead with this headset, compared to the old "Meta Quest 2".

text:
Thanks for the honest review.
It is hard to think who this is meant for other than mainstream VR users with an extra bit of cash or who are clueless about the competition. It is by no means directed towards hardcore VR enthusiasts (including Simmers). It is also not a next-gen VR.
Hardcore/NextGen - > Think:
- Foveated Rendering (Fail)
- Resolution of >= 2560x2560 per eye (Fail)
- MicroOLED/QLED/MicroLCD/MiniLED. (Fail)
- Valve Ecosystem tracking (Fail)
- Pancake/Aspheric lenses (Fail)
i.e. Next Gen VR -> Pimax Crystal*, Varjo *Aero*, Somnium Space *V1 & Big Screen Beyond (the latter still lacks fov/rendering)
So other than the Pancake lenses and slightly shy higher resolution (still less than the HP Reverb G2), this is so first-gen VR with a next-gen sample.
Lastly, I would not call this as New VR Standard as it is far from that. The Beyond probably you could say that but not this.

author: @SteveBUK73

text:
I asked Google Bard to give me a comparison between the Quest 3 and the PSVR2: PS VR2

Pros:
Higher resolution per eye (2000x2040 vs 1920x1800)
AMOLED display with HDR support
Haptic feedback in the headset and controllers
Eye tracking
Access to a large library of PS5 games (including some PS VR2 exclusives)
Cons:
Requires a PS5 to function
More expensive than the Quest 3
Wired headset
Quest 3

Pros:
Standalone headset, does not require a PC or console
More affordable than the PS VR2
Wireless headset
Backward compatible with Quest and Quest 2 games
Cons:
Lower resolution per eye than the PS VR2
LCD display without HDR support
No haptic feedback in the headset
No eye tracking
Smaller library of standalone games
Overall, the PS VR2 is the more powerful and feature-rich headset, but it is also more expensive and requires a PS5. The Quest 3 is a more affordable and versatile headset, but it sacrifices some performance and features.

Which one should you buy?

If you are already a PS5 owner and want the best possible VR experience, then the PS VR2 is the way to go. It offers higher resolution, better display technology, and more features than the Quest 3. However, if you are on a budget or want a headset that you can use without a PC or console, then the Quest 3 is a better option.

It is also worth considering your game library preferences. If you are interested in playing PS VR2 exclusives, then that headset is the only choice. However, if you are happy with the selection of standalone games available for the Quest, then the Quest 3 is a good option.

Ultimately, the best way to decide which headset is right for you is to consider your budget, gaming preferences, and existing hardware.

text:
11:15 The Quest 3 has a battery is roughly 3.6 amp hours. Assuming ~5 volt batteries we get 18 watt-hours.
From 18 watt hours we get ~ 9 watts of average power draw assuming a 2 hour playtime.

But here's the problem - A USB 3.x port can only provide around 5 watts of power, and the max a normal USB C can provide is 15 watts (but often motherboards don't support this full amount).

If you're stuck on a USB 3.x port, you'll only get like 3.5 hours of playtime even with your headset plugged in if my napkin maths are somewhat accurate.

text:
If I get one, I'll be getting controller straps, VR stand, new faceplate, new strap, headphones, and a lens cover. So that's around $150 beyond the price.
I play PCVR, and an annoying thing that Quest headsets do under OpenXR is warp the coloring (they are brightened and appear washed out) - I hate that and it annoys me. To correct it the programmer has to gamma the colors darker before scene submission so that the warping sort of restores them back to their original color, but that makes the darker colors VERY banded under OpenXR. This doesn't happen under OpenVR. I have no idea why their headsets do that.
I guess people are having issues with the microphone - and videos aren't discussing that enough.
