text:
Ya, IDK. If all you would use it for is PC VR, then it may not be worth it to upgrade. Because then all you are really getting is some improved clarity, a slight FOV bump, and perhaps some better comfort out of the box. I think the Q2 is great for PC VR for what it can do. It's what I mainly use it for.

However, personally, I'm looking beyond just PC VR and more toward the mixed reality/augmented reality aspect of the new headset. If that doesn't interest you, then I don't think it's worth upgrading to it at the moment. Of course, I don't have the Q3 in my hands yet, so I may change my opinion once I do, later this week.

author: @VirtualRealityOasis

text:
All Quest 3 changes (No particular order):

* Split headstrap for even weight distribution
* More adjustable headstrap
* Wireless charging
* More haptics/ Trigger Force Points
* No tracking ring
* Pancake lenses (thinner, better weight distribution)
* Wider FOV
* Higher resolution (>20%)
* IPD adjustment wheel (more precise, can adjust whilst wearing)
* Higher IPD range
* Easily adjust interface/headset distance to allow for glasses & FOV adjustment
* Better placed buttons
* Snapdragon XR2 Gen 2 (MASSIVE performance/graphical improvements)
* WiFi6E support
* 8GB RAM
* Much better speakers
* Full colour pass through, 10x higher resolution, can actually read phone screen
* Depth sensor (Allows for eye focus & automatic guardian & room setup)
* Augments (not confirmed for Q2/Pro, almost widgets for play space, virtual portals to games, screens, trophies, objects etc)
* Better battery & charging
* 128GB & 512GB storage options
* Asgard’s Wrath 2 Free Download with 128GB version
* 6 Months of Quest+ and Asgard’s Wrath 2 Free Download with 512GB version
* Referrals to get ≈£23 each in store credit per headset, £4 per app
* Xbox Cloud Gaming (not confirmed for Q2/Pro, allows for Xbox games to be played wirelessly on virtual monitor in MR)
* YouTuber referrals to get £15/£25 store credit for 128GB/512GB versions (limited time)
* Automatic Resolution increase on all games
* Prices: 128GB = £479.99 512GB = £619.99
* Inside Out Body Tracking
* Button to eject controller case
* Can scan QR codes

If you’ve got a Quest 2 and don’t really use it or are fine with it, please don’t upgrade. It’s really not worth the £500 to get the newer model, bearing in mind they don’t have a trade in system (yet 🤞🏻)
But if you’re an enthusiast or are addicted to VR, often share with family or just, rich, then it’s a great upgrade!

text:
Quest 3 is definitely worth - but with a 5090 and focus on PC games + movies, you may want to look at a dedicated PCVR headset. The Bigscreen Beyond 2 sounds right up your alley - great PCVR performance and OLED screens for watching movies (you'll want those crispy blacks and saturated colors). It's only like $900 for the regular version and $1,200 if you want eye tracking for social games/dynamic foveated rendering (keep in mind you'll need external trackers + controllers, so that adds to the price).

Pimax Crystal Super is also amazing - a bit more expensive at ~$1,800 - but doesn't need external trackers and comes with controllers.. so kinda events out with the cost of the BSB2 (but the tracking + controllers are worse than what you can get with the BSB budget)

text: Does this review still holds for 2025?
author: @darkromano_

text:
For the most part! Meta has made a lot of quality of life improvements over the past (almost) 2 years. IMO they've done a great job at releasing regular updates.

TLDR; still worth it and still the best bang-for-your buck headset on the market. It has a little bit of everything VR has to offer at the moment which is insane for the price point.

They've improved on most things: you can open virtual windows/browsers INSIDE of immersive experiences and games now, hand tracking is better, passthrough quality is slightly better, room mapping seems more accurate, battery life is a little better, they just revamped 3DoF mode, they've added travel/lay down modes, and a bunch more.. and they just recently revamped the whole UI which is nicer and more organized. I'm working on a 2-year update video to run through everything that has changed.

They still haven't released Augments or any meaningful mixed reality experiences - so it feels like mixed reality is struggling a bit and still a bit of a let down (unless you try out one of the new multiplayer games, which are admittedly quite buggy and not made by Meta). I wouldn't buy it for mixed reality unless you're just wanting to use it to watch moves/browse the web/etc; don't expect anything mindblowing there - it's more of a secondary feature that's nice to have.

text: 11:30 you may notice that the controllers appear to be moving before the hands rather than after. this is because the discrepancy you see in passthrough is the headset camera latency, which is noticeable, and not controller tracking latency, which is not
author: @Passw0rdYT

text:
I have another comment on this that I go into much more detail in, if you have a search plugin to dig that up!

I said it backwards than intended in the video, no doubt.

However, with more testing we found it's the opposite of latency (technically a mix of both). We discovered the controllers seem to be predicting where they're going to be in advance. We were able to test this by using a board perpendicular to our body, but you can try it with a door or a wall or something like that. When you move your hand into the wall, you will note that the rendered controller goes beyond the wall, even though your hand stopped much sooner. The controller continues forward (and will jump back when it realizes your hand stopped). It's seemingly trying to predict where your hand will be in the future to compensate and is doing so quite aggressively.

Latency definitely exists, but said latency seems low enough that it's not nearly enough to be causing the massive difference we see between the two. We ran a bunch of latency and reaction tests as well to try to confirm this - and while I can't possibly be 100% certain due to the limitations of our tests, I'm highly convinced the prediction engine is doing most of the work here to reduce processing (so there isn't as much latency to be had) and potentially counteract existing processing latency.

Visual quality of the lenses on the Quest 3 is still superior than 2/3S, but yea you don't need the hardware inside in that case. I'd argue that the wireless capability is worth it in both cases. author: @LeeRoss-bw3yd

text:
Meta recommends against it because the sun can fry the headset if it makes contact with the lenses for long enough.. think of a magnifying glass melting ants - except with your screens.

Otherwise, it does work outside and many people play in open fields (multiplayer is quite fun in this instance). If it's too sunny/bright, then tracking will not work because the headset can't see the controllers due to all the competing light.

TLDR; yes, but be careful with the lenses/buy lens covers to avoid the sun hitting them.

Google some tips on how to manage it. There's a lot of good info out there. Also, most games have "training wheels" that you can enable for motion sickness and they make it obvious in the settings. I still get motion sick in games like Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, where I'm flying across a city really quickly and looking around a lot, but nearly every other experience I've gotten used to after just a couple weeks of playing.

text: Is the resolution for watching movies/Youtube/Wander on Quest 3 remarkably better than Quest 2?
author: @richardcieply

text:
Yes - but I would argue the resolution isn't the primary factor, rather the new pancake lenses are. It makes the image so much clearer, with a better edge-to-edge clarity. For example, when watching a large screen (theatre size) movie on the Quest 2/3S, the edges of the frame are blurry and distorted and you have to look around to see everything clearly, but on the Quest 3 you can just use your eyeballs to look down. It's less like looking through a glass bottle and more like you're actually in the theatre room.

The resolution definitely helps as well, though - it's just hard to separate it from the insane new lenses. Be sure to grab the "Quest Games Optimizer" app - it's $10 but is a must-have - it will allow you to force your video playing app to full panel resolution (by default many apps scale lower to increase performance/save battery).

author: @NerdCrave

text:
I'd challenge you to not think so black-and-white. Think of it like a phone: you can make your phone battery last 3 days (by turning down your screen refresh rate, brightness, performance/sleep settings, etc.).. but if you actually use your phone to its full potential, and use it a lot, you're only going to get 1 day max (probably not even).

The same applies here. If you turned it up to the actual screen resolution, set your brightness, upped your refresh rate, turned off "battery saving" mode, etc.. then you'll get a better visual experience at the cost of running out of battery quicker.

Also, you might get fatigued now, but it's like working out - you will be able to last longer and longer as you play more. There's a reason everyone suggests getting one of the 3-4-hour battery strap extensions.

text: Is in worth is if you use in for normal work, like Word, PowerPoint and mail? I dont have the room for multiple screens and would like to know if this is a good option😄
author: @johndeer470

text:
It's *OK*. Working in VR is awesome!! But, with the Quest 3, I find that my eyes get strained pretty quickly due to the way text is rendered on the screen.. so if you're doing a lot of reading and writing (Word, Mail, etc.), then it's good for like 1-2 hour sprints before I need to take a break.

Working in VR is super fun and can help keep you much more focused than you would be outside the headset, and the Quest 3 is good enough for that, but the resolution and text aliasing is just a bit too low to be a great experience. I find it's fatiguing to read text for extended periods. Everything else is fine.

On the other hand, the Apple Vision Pro is perfect (it's just way too expensive for what it offers). If work is your primary use case, I'd wait for the Immersed Visor, Pimax Dream, or Samsung/Google XR headsets in 2025. All should have 4k resolution screens with much better tect display. They will be a little more expensive - the Visor is $1,000, for example - but are worth that extra cost if you're using it daily for your job.

author: @Dthaps

text:
@@Dthaps - Yea, that's very true! IPD is a major factor and if you don't fit in perfectly to one of the pre-set ranges on the 3S (or are outside the min/max), then your visuals get *even worse*. I had a whole explainer in my upcoming video about that, but removed it because the video is long and I'm not sure if people are gonna make it that far through / care enough.. but the underlying point stands.

I also have a video upcoming on headstraps! I've spent the past 6 months testing all the (battery only) straps and playing with the comfort levels. None of them are super comfortable when laying down (except for the original, which people would argue wasn't comfortable to begin with). If you need lay-down comfort, you'll probably want one WITHOUT a battery and just plug into the power bank - like you suggested. Find one that has the roundest/flattest back (the support behind your head). I didn't test any without a battery on them so I can't make a recommendation.

If you need the battery attached, the BoboVR S3 Pro (10,000mAh battery - this battery is hot swappable and held on by magnets, it can come off if you're laying down and moving a lot) and the Kiwi Designs (6,400mAh battery - battery is built-in so doesn't have that problem) are the most comfortable, they have a bracket that embraces the back of your head, but the plastic still sticks out of the back and makes it somewhat uncomfortable. Your best bet for laying down with a battery strap is to look for a softer pillow TBH - the softer pillow you can get, the less it "pushes" into the back of your skull. I have one of those cheap fake feather pillows and it works perfectly for this. None are really comfortable for laying on your side - the weight is just "off" because of the headset on your face.

Also, wild suggestion, but the Apple Vision Pro strap is actually quite comfortable when laying down - because you don't need the strap on the top of your head. It's $100 though and you'd need a 3D Printer to print the attachment arms for it. Maybe check Amazon for a Quest 3 strap that tries to mimic that style!

text: When I had it plugged in my pc it didn’t seem to charge unless I stopped playing…any thoughts on that?
author: @Itsmepreshap

text:
This likely means your computer's USB port can't ouput enough power. I think the default for non-PD plugs is like 4.5w - which simply isn't enough, you need closer to 30w.

You could try another port on your PC to see one delivers more power OR pop on Amazon and search for a USB-C cable with "PD" (power delivery) injection - this will allow you to plug the port into your PC as you are now and then also plug in a charging brick to deliver power to your headset. You can get the cables for like $6 or the hubs for like $20. Make sure you use a 30w power brick or above to put power into it, I'd suggest 45w - the brick that comes with your Quest 3 won't be enough.

Hope that helps!
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text: @@aline.tech.reviews how do I check the output wattage?
author: @Itsmepreshap

text: You can either look up your motherboard model OR you can get a cable (or a USB-C device) that tests output power.  I suggest the second option just because it's a handy tool to have and should only cost a few bucks - you can use it to see the power draw of any USB-C device you have.

text:
Battery video is almost completed! It took a long time to test them all, but I'm finally done and working on the script.

For trialing, I would suggest buying from Amazon if you are in the US (if not, check your Amazon holiday policy). The return policiy for all purchases this time of year extends you through January 31, 2025. That's a much longer return period (2+ months if you bought today) vs. the normal 15-day policy. If you find you have balance issues, grab a "BoboVR S3 Pro" or "M3 Max" headstrap. This distributed the weight across your head 20x better than the strap that comes with it, and it has a battery on the back that acts as a counter-balance (a good thing). As a spoiler, it's my battery winner for wearable battery.

Seated play is extremely intuitive and most people I've seen that play for long hours do so seated. There is a "stationary" space mode that allows you to remain seated, and you can simply use the joystickd on the controllers to do stuff like turn around (I use controllers for that when standing a lot as well, so it's totally normal and not a workaround by any means - its intended to work this way). VR headsets are becoming more common for elderly, and being used in elderly homes as a form of entertainment - so it's definitely a viable and tested use case to be seated and have a great experience. Meta/Apple products are typically very accesible devices - Meta a bit less so but they still have a ton of accessibility options to assist with motion sickness, disabilities, etc.

There's also a new "lay down" mode you can set, so you can play completely prone in bed.

I hope that helps! Please don't hesitate to reply if you have other questions or require clarification.

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews Thank you for still watching your comments.

I have a Quest 3, Samsung Odyssey+, and Valve Index, so this is my first foray into Metaface land. When it comes to the Samsung controllers they are FAR beyond anything else in VR. They're touchpads like the Steamdeck as well as being clickable like the PS5 controller. What looks like an unusable disk can actually have 8 inputs mapped to it vs the 2 buttons that could use that space.

Don't get me wrong, I love the Metaface 3's pancake lenses and razor sharp display even though it doesn't have Samsung's deep blacks and vibrancy of its OLED, but those controllers are the worst I've ever used in a decade of using VR.

author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews The G2 controllers are similar to Meta controllers. Good luck finding Odyssey controllers though. They're expensive and most people just buy Reverb ones if they need a replacement since Samsung EOL'd them. WMR headsets and controllers are compatible so technically you were in the same ecosystem as Samsung.

But reading your comment made me just go compare them more closely to realize why I prefer the Samsung ones more and there's 2 big differences. The biggest one is where your grip sits. The contour puts the controller a full 3/4" deeper into your palm vs being in your fingers. This puts the disk and analog stick perfectly under the middle of my thumb and the trigger rests past the first knuckle of my index finger. Whereas the Meta controllers have both buttons under my finger TIPS, even though the overall controller is shorter. It reminds me of the first gen XBox controllers where people quickly started importing the Asia ones because the US ones were uncomfortably fat.

But you don't need a Samsung controller to feel what I mean. Simply pick up a PS4 controller vs your Meta one and you'll realize Sony also puts the controls under your fingers, not at their tips. Purposely slide the PS controller partially out of your palm and there's the Meta controller. Which probably explains why I felt like I was going to drop the Meta controllers before adding the strap covers.

The other is that while the Meta controller feels high quality, that analog stick feels terrible when you're directly comparing. It's too small and heavily sprung to give precision control on par with the Samsung, PS, or XB controllers. I noticed this on Moss: Book 2 while trying to navigate bridges and kept falling off if I tried to move quickly at all.

I really think it's the years of playing Playstation that makes me prefer the Samsung cause you could convince me the same designer worked on both of them, or at the very least used the same design philosophy.

author: @deokorusu

text:
I believe the nose gap you are referring to is normal - if you see light coming from your nose bridge, but not the sides, then I believe you are within spec. You can buy a different "facial interface" from AMVR that will cover that spot better to block out light, but I like to keep it so I can see around me and make sure I'm not out of place.

Your blurriness is likely a different issue. For starters do you know your IPD? If not, you can go to like a lense crafter to get an exact number (they do it for free) or you can just turn the knob on the bottom of your Quest 3 until you are seeing most clearly.

From there, you should try to angle the headset up and down on tour face. Not push it up or down, but ANGLE or twist it up or down. For me, the sweet spot is angled slightly upward. Just keep playing with it until you get it right.

If you still don't have any luck from there, you might need glasses 🤓. You can lens inserts with your prescription on them

Let me know if any of that helps! It'll probably take you 30 minutes of fiddling to find your "sweet spot" - once you found it, remember it and tighten the heastrap.

You can also get headstraps that allow for easier adjustment than the default piece of cloth. I highly recommend the BoboVR M3 Max (I put a link in the vdieo description for you). It makes it eaiser to angle the headset on your face and is way more comfortavle + comes with a 3-4 hour battery.

author: @ramschasar7654

text:
Yea, I resonate with that - the constant tinkering definitely gets frustrating after a while. There's so many layers, from the networking and link apps to the individual game settings/mods, that I often find myself tweaking settings and troubleshooting for 6 hours... and I don't ever get to enjoy the game. The win is just the fact that you got it running.

I'll argue it's a primary reason VR hasn't taken off yet - there's just too much tinkering required to get the non-standalone games running. You can't even sit down and just hit play on what should be a plug-and-play experience for a lot of these sim racing games. Ports need to become more widely available and headsets need to integrate better with PC's to reduce that level of friction.

A comparison I can make is that of 3D printers. I had wanted a 3D printer for nearly a decade.. but each time I went shopping for one it was this entire process where you had to build the printer, mod it, and fiddle and tweak it just to get it printing basic items - and then having to do each print and tweak and test more to dial in all the settings. If you wanted a "real" printer, you had to upgrade nearly every part on your own. There's simply not enough time for a human to have to tinker every aspect of their lives; I needed a printer that I could plug in and start printing with... and that wasn't a viable solution until recently in 2022 when Bambu Labs released their X1 Carbon.

The Quest 3 is by far the closest - but we have such a long ways to go.

text:
Sure thing!

And yea, I definitely understand. If battery life/degradation is important to you, then you may want to play it safe. Meta says that to better ensure the life of their batteries and to prevent mass complaints if batteries do start to degrade.. but then sell their own battery head strap AND link cable (both of which charge while playing). 🤔

From my experience, battery degradation is a real thing but it will happen so slowly and minorly over time that you won't ever notice a real world change.. and if you're charging while you play you definitely won't notice. Most batteries now come coupled with advanced management systems to regulate charging and extend their life. Unless you're absolutely abusing with crazy temperatures and trying to overcharge it, you probably won't get more down to 80% life over a couple years. Most users will make it to the Quest 4 before their battery is a hindrance and we have enough evidence of this from Quest 2 batteries. Finally, if you use it so much that you really do a number on the battery, you likely got your money's worth and the repair cost or a new unit will be worth it to you.

With a battery strap like the BoboVR M3 Max, you'll get 4 hours of play time (in most experiences) and then can swap the battery out to get 2-3 more. That's plenty for even the most hardcore users. Without a battery, you'll often dip below 2 hours (for example, playing that Assasins Creed game at launch, I was barely getting 1 hour of playtime - which just isn't enough).

My suggestion would be to not let battery anxiety control how you use the device. Enjoy it and maybe just be cautious of overloading it to 100% and letting it sit there for extended periods to help combat your use.

text:
I have a 4070 Ti and if the MR stuff and battery life is not so great, does it make sense to buy the Quest 3 instead of a wired only headset?
Is there something convenient "around that price" that gives more VR quality in replacement of battery and MR stuff?
Can you use the Meta games etc. on other headsets using PC?
author: @Falkoni339

text:
It depends on what your use case is, ultimately. There is no "one headset does everything best", so you have to choose between the pros/cons for how you want to use it.

The Quest 3 is definitely the most bang for your buck.. you get all the standalone, mixed reality, and virtual reality features, with good visuals; plus, you can just plug it into your PC (USB-C) for power and data.. all for only $500 (which is insane). The battery isn't really an issue in most cases. If you're playing standalone games a lot, just buy a BoboVR S3 Pro headset with the 10,000 mAH battery - that gets you like 4-5 hours of gameplay and you can buy an extra battery if you need more; if you're playing PCVR, just plug it into a power source and it runs infinitely without draining battery. I think it's my top recommendation unless you are a heavy simmer or PCVR player.

PCVR headsets are more niche and you have to pick the one that best fits. You lose the standalone/mixed reality experience (in most cases) in trade for a more crisp/pleasant visual experience. The G2 Reverb would have been a great option, but it's now effectively dead so I wouldn't risk it unless you can get one suuuper cheap. Otherwise, the Pimax Crystal Light is a great PCVR headset (in terms of visuals) for ~$800, but they have a bit of QC issues right now as it's fairly fresh off the line. Bigscreen Beyond is meh IMO, it's light which is good but it's also $1,000, has no tracking built in, and you're very limited with movement (you can't really look around with your eyes, you have to move your head).. and then you have to spend another like $1,000 on base stations and controllers.. so it's a hard sell that I'd only recommend if you need the small form factor or watch movies a lot. HTC Vive Vision just released but they won't have displayport support for a while (meaning, if you buy it now you won't get it and they could still change their minds).

For PCVR, if your primary focus is 100% sim racing or flying, go for the Pimax Crystal Light. If you're on a budget, get a PICO Neo Link 3. Otherwise, I'd wait until something new comes out or get the Quest 3. You can get a Quest 2 used for like $100 right now, that might be the play and just wait for the next PCVR headset.

If it was me and I had to pick one today, I'd get a Pimax Crystal Light for PCVR, and a Quest 3 for everything else. Overall, I'd pick the Quest 3. That, or just wait.

text: I wonder if Meta Quest 3 is better than HTC Vive Pro 2 in PC gaming.
author: @TinyTiti

text:
I don't own the HTC Vive Pro 2, but I am confident enough to say having looked through the lenses that I much prefer the Quest 3 pancake lenses. Quest 3 still looks sharper, even though the Vive Pro 2 has a higher resolution. It's very hard to go back to Fresnel lenses after using Pancakes IMO; but if you've never tasted it, then you should be good to stay at Fresnels 😆

That said, there are definitely tradeoffs. Vive Pro 2 wins for sure in the latency and video feed - the Quest 3 has to compress the video to get to your headset which reduces quality and adds latency. Quest 3 though has built-in Wifi for wireless and is quite usable, Pro 2 requires an external module add-on (and is already more expensive).

I'd argue the HTC Vive Pro 2 is too old at this point. There are too many new headsets on the docket with pancake lenses + OLED/LED screens that are in production right now.. but even then, if you can deal with the potential QC issues, the Pimax Crystal Light is superior to the Vive Pro 2. The Bigscreen Beyond might even be, but I never got that one to try.

text: How is the screen door effect?
author: @taden2007

text:
Definitely exists and definitely noticeable. About the same-ish? as the Quest 2, like the lenses are much more crisp so you can see it better, even though the screen resolution is technically higher. Way better than older headsets if you've used any of those.

There's also a secondary factor - the screens are rotated in the Quest 3, which means the pixels are all diagonal.. I'm not sure it increases the SDE, but it definitely pronounces it or makes it stick out a bit more (especially true if you're trying to read text). It's like noticeable aliasing on top of the SDE.

Definitely "fades away" in games, Quest menus, and such.. I never really notice it once I'm immersed in a task. When watching movies, I sometimes notice it in bright scenes or when I'm trying to read something. However, in ImmersedVR or any type of work application, it is pretty noticeable if you're trying to focus on reading. I think generally you get used to it the longer you wear the headset and your brain almost starts to mask it out. If you care about productivity/work, 4k is the way - otherwise, I think the 2k Quest 3 screens are totally viable for everything else.

text:
I posted about this a lot in other comments that said the same thing:

1) I believe I jumbled my words in the video
2) After a lot of testing, the latency just isn't that high.. what seems to be happening is that the rendered controllers are "predicting" where they're going to be and jumping ahead. You can test this by putting an object in front of you, and running your hand into it. You will notice that the rendered controller goes throufh the object, where your hand stopped, and will then "jump" back.

It's likely a combonation of both, but the latency just doesn't appear to be high enough to cause this drastic effect alone

I did try the Quest 3 recently, as well as the PCVR2 (on PC) and I must say, especially for things like horror games, the OLED black levels are a deal-breaker. For example, in the game Phasmophobia, you get a sense of being completely inside the map. Whereas on the Quest 3, the blacks were so washed that it completely broke the immersion. If it wasn't for the poor mura on the PSVR2, I would have kept that. Any idea on what OLED headset is better than this?
author: @Hunterx777x

text:
Apologies for the delay! I was prepping and traveling for the Immersed Visor IRL event.. which, funnily enough, happens to be an OLED VR headset (but one that's focused on productivity/work and likely won't get OpenXR game adoption for a long while, assuming they recover from how poorly their event went).

Unfortunately, I don't know that there's a perfect option. The best currently seems to be the Bigscreen Beyond, but I've also seen many complaints about the lens distortions and glare (and coming from the other two, you're going to have a reduced FOV). On top of that, you will have to source trackers, controllers, etc. separately. I never bought one because of all the above (it's a whole process to even connect your knuckles controllers), and lack of eye tracking. You can't even get 6dof without setting up base stations.

If it were me, I'd wait for the next generation of headsets. Micro OLED is becoming more common place, with more manufacturers and increased success in production. Pimax has the Crystal Super coming out this year with an OLED panel option which is positioned to have the best visuals we've seen on any headset in the past. It seems a handful of other companies are also working on new OLED headsets now that it's feasible. If you can't wait, check out SadlyItsBradly's and OptimumTech's review on the Bigscreen Beyond to see if you are willing to accept those downsides.

text: is quest 2 to valve index a good upgrade? i know all the pros and cons and all that but im still not sure if i should
author: @UnderscoreOfficialIThink

text:
If you know all the pros and cons and all that, then you should have a pretty good idea on whether it's the move for you!

I wouldn't say the $750 price increase is worth it over a Quest 2, IMO. It's definitely an improvement, assuming you want to be wired and PCVR only, but $750 more is pushing it. The Index is outdated and never received a price drop; you'd need to have a specific and frequent use case to justify it. If you can find a used one, in good shape, with all the accessories for like $600 then it could be worth it over a Quest 3.

I'd go for the Pimax Crystal Light if I wanted dedicated PCVR for sim racing/etc. and didn't eye or fb tracking.. and I'd wait for the Pimax Crystal Super to launch this fall if I needed both (it's expensive - I'd go for for the Bigscreen Beyond and get lighthouse base stations + knuckle controllers if I couldn't afford it).

If you aren't deep into PCVR then the Quest 3 still seems like the best bang-for-your buck headset on the market currently. You just have to sacrifice that direct DP line for compressed video feed of your games. It's a jack of all trades and offers the best amount of versatility for the $, where all the other options lock you into a specific use case or form factor (and need additional accessories, like lighthouse stations).

text: would love to see a video comparing the Xreal AR glasses with the Quest 3, I wanted a headset primarily for watching movies on a big screen, I ended up ordering the Quest 3 cause I assumed the closed face pad will make the video quality better and more immersive, I wonder what the Xreal video watching experience is like
author: @The8merp

text:
That's a great idea, and a comparison I haven't thought to make a video about.

You are correct in that the Quest 3 is definitely more immersive. Not only because of the closed face pad, but also because of the increased field of view. The XREAL Air 2 Pro has a very, very tiny field of vie win comparison, so you really can only look straight ahead (and sometimes that means you have to move your eyes to clearly see the edge of the window that the movie is on). Also, with the XREALs, the glasses are transparent which can make it hard to actually see the details of a movie unless you use the blocker (but then the illusion of the 200 inch screen goes away and you're back to watching what feels like a normal screen in front of of).

So, immersion definitely goes to the Quest 3. However, form factor goes to XREAL. I'd never take a Quest 3 on a flight and sometimes I just don't want to wear it because I don't feel like having my face get hot. XREAL actually works really well on a flight - but the beam is necessary to get rid of all the jitters or else I got a tiny bit motion sick riding on the plane and watching a movie.

The color in the XREALs is much better IMO and there's 0 screen door effect. Blacks are transparent though, which can be confusing unless you're wearing the blockers. Quest 3 colors seem muddied in comparison and you can see the gaps between the pixels (BUT, the one place I never actually notice them is when I'm immersed in a movie - so that might be a moot point here). Also, the XREAL has a weird effect I don't like where the edges of the screen have like green/red blurry lines round them - presumably from the reflections and overlap.

Overall, I'd pick the Quest 3 any day unless I traveled a lot. In fact, and I'm going to spoil my XREAL review here lol, the only use cases I've found for the XREAL are traveling or playing a gaming console, like the Nintendo switch, when you don't have a TV nearby (like in my bedroom).

I've made a note of this topic to make a video on in the future. I have a lot in the queue to get done first but I will do my best to get that done.. I should be able to cover it in like 5-8 minutes I think.

text: Hey! Hope it's not a big ask but could you elaborate on the fitness aspect? This is gonna be my first VR headset but I'm actually getting it as I want to spend more time up on my feet while still enjoying games (I spend far too much time at my desk, basically). Are there any free fitness apps on the quest 3 and are there any you've tried that you think are particularly good? Any suggestions for games where exercise is involved are greatly appreciated too. I've heard beatsaber is one. I have a high-end gaming PC so can take steam suggestions with VR compatibility too.
author: @mimii694

text:
Hey there! Most definitely, I've tried a ton of the apps, but I admittedly don't work out in VR regularly.

There's a handful of "free ones" but as far as I know they're all demos that effectively harass you until you sign up for a subscription or buy something. "FitXR" is a free one I tried that lets you work out in mixed reality and is quite nice - but they want you to buy the subscription.

The best "free" option would probably be to make sure you have access to treadmill or stationary bike IRL, then load up 180/360 walking or biking videos and just enjoy the ride along those.

One cheap option is "The Thrill of the Fight" if you're into boxing. It's only $10 and is a very simple gameplay look, but boxing those guys will definitely get your heart pumping after just a few minutes lol.

Other cheap games like Eleven Table Tennis, Superhot, or Pistolwhip are all fun games that have you moving around a lot.. so you do get winded and sweaty after a bit of playing them. I'd check them out to see if you like the gameplay.

"Les Mills Body Combat" is best exercise experience I've had by far, and it's the most polished of all the exercise options - but it's $30 (be sure to use discount codes, you can use someone else's code for 20% off any game). If you're going to use it daily, that seems worth it.

Outside of that, I've found any first-person shooter like Contractors / Population: One, etc. are my go-to. I get immersed enough to where I'm like crouching around and walking a bit while moving my arms a lot.. to me, it's a good mix of light exercise while having a good time.. Superhot/etc. are a level of exercise up from that, but I find them boring after playing a couple times.

I hope that helps.

text: Can I use it while charging, so that I can use it for a long time
author: @solocede

text:
Yes, you can! Get a 35W+ charger and a quality (and long) cable. Anker makes great charging bricks and cables. You can also use power banks, if you want to stick a power bank in your pocket or a backpack (to avoid being tethered to the wall). And, if you want to go fully mobile, there are headstraps like the BoboVR S3 that have a 10,000mAh battery attached (that you can hot swap) - I'm getting like 5-6+ hours with this headstrap and I can just swap the battery out with a fresh one if I want to.

I'd also suggest a right-angle cable at one end, a straight cable can put extra stress on the USB-C port of the headset when you are moving your head around.

text: Question 2: Since Mac does not offer VR games (that I know), what would be a good small form factor PC rig for VR gaming?
author: @AJCoReTV

text:
I don't know that I would suggest one of the small form-factor PC's - those little boxes you see won't have a graphics card powerful enough to run PCVR (you need a good one to render the game for each eye and with a solid FPS).

You would want to build an "ITX" (small form factor motherboard) gaming PC. Companies like NZXT sell them called the "H1 Mini". Ideally, you would get a 4080 if you can for future proofing yourself for many years. Otherwise, a 4060 is fine.

You will save a lot of money by building one yourself. There is a Reddit sub dedicated to helping others build PCs. You can go there and tell them you need help building a "ITX gaming PC for PCVR with Quest 3" and someone will send you a list of the parts you should get that will give you the best bang for your buck. Then, you can just go buy all those parts from Amazon/Newegg and build it yourself.
author: @aline.tech.reviews

(I will also prob buy PSVR2 once the PS5 pro comes out, so my son won’t feel left out of my Quest 3).

text:
11:30 I think it's important to note here that the tracking isn't lagging behind, the passthrough video feed is.
The controllers are tracked very precisely, but turning the photos from all of those cameras into a 3D experience a hundred times a second takes some processing
author: @Rocco-tb9ih

text:
Check out the other comments on this! I spoke backwards in the video, but that lead to us doing lot of testing, because the passthrough cameras didn't seem to be that laggy. It seems like what's happening is a combination, but the largest noticeable piece seems to be that the controllers are being projected into the future - it's trying to predict where they're going to be. Maybe to counteract the slight passthrough lag? To confirm this, we put a board in front of us and then ran our hand into it - the physical controller stops, but the virtual version keeps going through the board and then "snaps back" into place when it catches up.

So definitely true, but it seems to be a combination of the two causing the issue.

author: @codyeomindset

text:
You can definitely do all those things! And yes, you can access YouTube VR videos, DeoVR videos, and download any 3D or VR video you find online to play within your headset.

There are tons of travel videos featuring different countries and cities, hiking and biking trips through nature trails or scenic routes, and even drone videos that give you a bird's eye view of everything. Many are even "interactive", where someone guides you through what you're looking at as they explore a city. It's all quite fun. However, there are a couple of limitations:
1) The camera equipment/resolution required to get an awesome 360-degree video experience that's also crisp is expensive, and very few people have access to it, so there isn't a lot of "perfect" content. Most content is fuzzy or blurry because the camera technology hasn't caught up yet. However, 180-degree videos are much more accessible and can be just as interactive.. you just can't turn around; you are always looking ahead and in your peripheral.
2) You don't get to have any control over the experience because they are pre-recorded videos, so you are kind of a third-party observer.

You might not think you're into games, but there are many designed specifically for entertainment and traveling in 3D worlds. Games like Wander and Google Earth VR, meditation and relaxation games, Museum of Other Realities (a virtual art show), and so on.. there are tons of museum and art exhibits that have VR counterparts. Plus, if you want to try an actual game, Skyrim VR and No Man's Sky offer tons of exploration in beautiful fantasy worlds. This technology exist in many different fields and sectors.. you can join live events and conferences.. and many games let you present your made objects to other people to share or exhibit. Games like VR Chat let users build entire worlds they can explore with one another. "Game" is a very loose definition.

The Apple Vision Pro and the Apple team are actually making major pushes here with extremely high-quality videos. Some of their new interactive videos are INSANELY IMPRESSIVE. I wouldn't recommend buying an AVP for that just yet because there are so few, but just know that as 4k VR headsets become more available (which is literally happening this year and next year) the experiences will be more plentiful and higher quality.

I hope that all helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.

text: I would like to sell my quest 2 and upgrade to quest 3.  Can my apps transfer over?
author: @Jefff72

text:
I haven't tried this directly, but as far as I understand as long as you are logging in with the same Meta account, you should be good to go. Your app profiles and game saves are on the cloud, not the device itself. Once you login to the new device with that account you should have everything populate to where it starts downloading it all from the cloud. AFAIK, there are a handful of games that the save files might not transfer for - if you are concerned about that you should look up each individual game.

However, I don't think your images/videos/photos/etc. will transfer - you will need to back those up first.

text: It would make more sense if they just removed the virtual controllers in passthrough and kept the lines since you can see the controllers anyways
author: @slicethebread4117

text:
IIRC, the issue here is twofold, 1) occlusion for hands wasn't a thing, all real world objects always appeared behind virtual objects, meaning your hands would disappear behind windows if you reached out.
I know they've technically got hand occlusion working to some extent, but I haven't been in MR for a while to test that it's live and actively working full-time or if it's still in development mode, and 2) there is a latency that exists between the movement of the two and the virtual versions seem to be trying to project where you're going next to 'keep up' with all the real-time processing. This also exists so you can see what the system things your hands are doing, like pinching, in the event you might not be intending to actually pinch with your fingers.

I agree with you if they can make it happen flawlessly, but it felt like those 2 things were limits the last time I was playing around with the Quest 3.

text: So can you review how you use this hardware in a music production setting?
author: @FourthMatrix

text:
Unfortunately, I don't think that I'm qualified. 😄 I'm not a music producer and I don't often get to play with all my midi toys.. it's more like a hobby that I jam out with once in a blue moon. I typically use only the midi controllers and NI's Maschine software for live music looping. I'd love to get deeper into those workflows, but there just aren't enough hours in a day for other priorities.

I can't see much of a use case for it there outside the virtual screens. It could be helpful in learning an instrument if you can find an app to map notes to your IRL instrument, and there are some fun virtual music apps like drums you can play - they're more for entertainment than real production, though I'm sure you could produce something listenable.

author: @G_man_

text:
No, unfortunately still not comfortable in the Quest 3. It is usable for a bit of time, maybe 2-4 hours max, but it really does cause a lot of eye strain and discomfort for the same reasons you've described and because you can still see individual pixels (and the diagonal rotation of them causes some weird aliasing and edges - it hurts my eyes to read basic emails). It's clearer than the Quest 2, but not clear enough. I'd argue it's okay for watching movies or video editing (not color correcting) but reading, programming, etc. all hurts.

I've also got the Apple Vision Pro and it is ACTUALLY clear and usable. If working with virtual screens is your primary use case, I'd wait.. there's a bunch of upcoming headsets with 4k microOLED screens that won't cost an arm and a leg like the AVP. The Immersed Visor is finally shipping this year - I'm going to go demo it in Austin in September so I'll be able to comment on how clear theirs is. Samsung, Google, and a handful of others have 4k headsets on the way. AVP 2.0 is apparently in the works for general consumers.

4k+ is the screen resolution you need per eye, IMO, for this use case.

text: My air link or cable link doesn't work /: Idk what to do, any ideas what might be the problem? Thanks!
author: @FoxeyWind

text:
I could be a lot of things.. let's start with the cable.

1) Are you using the official cable or a third party one?
2) Did you download the Quest Link or other software on your PC? If not, search google for "quest 3 link cable instructions" and follow the instructions to download the app on your computer and get set up. There are a few steps you need to take to get it set up for the first time.
3). If you've done #2, what error message or what behavior are you getting that you can describe so we can help you find the issue?

text: ​@@aline.tech.reviews I just found a USB-C To USB-A in my house since I heard I should use that. I have the oculus link software on my PC and also I have a quest 2 (Maybe that helps). 😄 I do not get any errors, my oculus just can't find my PC
author: @FoxeyWind

text:
It's possible that your cable is not compatible - I believe you will want a USB3.0 cable that can support 5gbps. Do you have anothrr cable or a USB-C cable or a USB-A that supports data and power?

Second thing to check would be to make sure the USB ports on your PC are USB3. If you're using ones on the back of your motherboard, it's possible most of them are 2.0 and only 1 is 3.0 (which should be a different color). If you can, use USB-C to eliminate this possibility.

text: I have a meta quest 2, I feel like everything I see is behind a veil of blurriness/grain/aliasing. Is this still true on meta quest 3?
author: @whatisthat7539

text:
I would argue that all headsets experience this to some degree, especially in high contrast scenes where you can see the lenses themselves with the god rays and particles on them, but the Quest 3 feels much less and is a big improvement over the 2 in that regard.

The upgrade to pancake lenses and the upgraded resolution of the displays will eliminate a some of the aliasing (you can still see individual pixels because the lenses are so clear and the PPD isn't high enough). There's not much blurring that I can perceive in normal use (unless I'm in a high contrast scene with black background and white text - at which point the rays and diffusion cause a blurriness).

After finishing the Half Life Alyx masterpiece, every game I install and try feels boring and dull. The social aspect (metaverse) that Meta tries to shove into your face every second is just annoying. Basically, the only thing left to do is to watch 3d movies and vr porn lol. The technology is top notch, apart from the passthrough lenses, but the content is waaay too dull and niche.
author: @ozzless

text:
Appreciate that!

I've definitely heard that a lot... users feeling let down after finishing Half Life: Alyx because no other titles come close. I've even seen some people telling others to save Alyx until absolutely last when they're completely bored of the headset to avoid it lol

It's also unfortunate that companies like Ubisoft (using Ubisoft as an example here because of their statements about Assassin's Creed: Nexus) have determined VR isn't worth investing in right now because sales didn't meet expectations and they can't justify making more games until the market grows.. and that was presumably after getting funding from Meta to build the game on their platform. If you don't have that game yet, it still pales in comparison to Alyx, but it is the closest standalone game I've found.

We're going to need indie devs to save the day.. and they're going to need kickstarter campaigns for funding. I feel like we should be getting close to that as VR is becoming more popular and the next generations of developers are taking XR/AR classes.

I imagine the VR gaming audience must be quite small.. as I know myself and a lot of people I know are into VR for the daily computing aspect or pure simming (car/plane/etc. via a PC).

I'm sure you're going to love it - also, for when you get it set up, check out Assetto Corsa with the VR mods.. it takes a while to get everything set up, but it's awesome too.

RE: charging, it depends on your computer. If your computer has a high-wattage USB-C output that also supports data transfer, then you just need a basic USB-C link cable. However, if you don't have that (or if you don't know if you have that), then you will want to get a powered link cable (I can't put links here, search "quest 3 link cable with power" on Amazon - it's a USB-C cable with 2 ends, 1 goes into your PC and 1 goes into a charger brick.. and it juices your headset with extra power from the charging brick while also giving the data from the PC). It might not keep you you at 100% all the time, but you should be getting like 12-18 hours of play time which is basically the same thing because no one plays that long.

author: @MrChucklepuff

text:
You could try a few things: 1) open the virtual menu and look at the text, make sure you can also read that - if not, make sure that the headset is snug on your head then use the IPD dial (the wheel on the bottom of the headset) to adjust the distance of the lenses until the text looks crisp to you. For me, I had to tilt the headset up a bit on my head and snug it down tight to get the best clarity. 2) make sure you are updated to the latest firmware, v64 fixed a lot of the "blowout" issues that cause screens like your phone to become extremely blown out, and 3) if you still can't read them, try turning down the brightness on your phone to see if that helps (it's what allowed me to read my phone before the updates).

I hope something there helps you out! If not, I am unsure of what the cause would be.. but would suggest checking out Reddit or submitting a support request

author: @DavidWasTakn

text:
Appreciate that!

I find the Wi-Fi connection to be more than just sufficient.. I prefer to use it in most cases (unless I want to load up a seated or sim racing game, or get the best graphics quality) because the wireless provides a new level of freedom and you don't have to worry about the cable getting in your way. The latency isn't all that noticeable and the decreased quality doesn't bug me much. I had to get a dedicated router to get this performance.

The wired connection, however, is objectively the best experience (especially in your case with a 5900x + RTX 4080, because you can max out all the settings with a high bitrate, high resolution, 90hz, and so on that might cause more latency and compression issues with wireless). I also prefer using the cable for work apps like ImmersedVR as I don't get annoying mouse lag.

Your PC will be used to render the graphics and export the video in both cases. Wired just allows it to do so at higher fidelity due to more bandwidth and less wireless nonsense.

In the end, the option to be tethered OR wireless when you want is an awesome feature that the Quest 3 provides - which really ups its bang-for-buck IMO.

text: @@aline.tech.reviews Thanks so much for the really detailed response dude, I'm getting fibre internet soon too so hopefully I wont have any issues with WiFi, if so I'll have to stick to wired but it's probably just one of those situations where I need to try it and find out. Will also have to go to a local place to demo VR to see if I don't get motion sickness, but thanks a lot for the insight :)
author: @DavidWasTakn

text:
@@DavidWasTakn - You're very welcome!

The WiFi connection created here will be local like this: from your PC -> to your router -> to your headset. In other words, it never makes contact with the outside world so the fiber internet won't give you an advantage here unless you are playing multiplayer games that connect externally (fiber internet is awesome outside that, though, so congrats - you'll never be able to go back after you experience those download speeds!!). For this integration, what you need is a good/dedicated router - specifically a "6e" router. Having one dedicated for this use case, that isn't also used for your phone, smart lights, etc. will give you the best results. A router is an extra $100+ cost, though, when you can get a good PCVR link cable for like $15.

And great call on going to test one out first! If you feel like you get motion sickness, try to play with the settings - most games have settings for teleporting movement and vignetting on the edges to reduce motion sickness.. this may help you get used to it more quickly.

Definitely let me know if you end up getting it or not and how your experience went!

The Quest 3 is definitely a visual upgrade from the Valve Index, but if you're not going to take advantage of the wireless streaming (which does have artifacts and latency), mixed reality/passthrough, and standalone apps, then you're leaving a lot on the table and agreed those 'upgrades' become minor.

It not only raises the bar for devs, but also increases the adoption rate of VR in general.. I've been on the sidelines watching the VR world unfold for just over a decade and I've never seen this level of hype for what's next.

If you aren't in a rush and have the budget, the Pimax Crystal Super is on the way with 2 interchangeable headset options; and, if you're on a budget, their Crystal Light is going to drop in like a week or so for only $700-900 depending on the variation you choose (both of which you can continue to use with your existing controllers and lighthouse setup). I pre-order the Super to review since it won't be out until later this year; I may pick up the Crystal Light here as well if I manage to catch up on my backlog of videos in time. Both seemed like a crazy value for the money, but I've never used a Pimax device before (they were going to send me the Crystal to test but then didn't when they realized I needed more than 2 weeks to review it).

text: The "novelty wears off" speech isn't really true for VR/AR headset since its potential haven't even reached 2% yet due to lack of softwares. author: @toututu2993

text:
To clarify, I didn't say VR/AR is a novelty item or that the novelty wore off in the sense of VR/AR headsets! I'm a long-time VR supporter and am looking forward to the future. I did, however, say that the novelty fades quickly in relation to MR (mixed reality), especially in its current state - it's simply an alpha/testing feature in the Quest 3.. meaning, there is no real application for it outside testing the tech and a few novel experiences, like First Encounters (which were built to showcase and test the tech).

I also said that I bought the 128gb version, over the 512, because I don't anticipate I'll utilize that much space, because many VR apps don't have a ton of replayability due to simple gameplay loops or focus on a single gimmicky VR feature (this does not imply that all games do this, but enough to where the extra storage space isn't necessary to hoard them).

I CTRL+F'd my script for each time I said the word novelty and those were the only two instances.

Many people have already let their Quest 3 to rot on a shelf untouched.. myself included, I haven't picked it up unless I wanted to try a newly released game or test battery headstraps for an upcoming video. I even heard SadlyItsBradley say in a video that he hasn't picked up his Quest 3 since his review video (outside to check something). It's not bad by any stretch and I'm still recommending it as the best bang-for-your-buck headset in terms of functionality vs. value. However, when you have use cases outside of gaming (or prefer games with hi-fidelity graphics) and have other headsets, the Quest 3's mixed reality isn't all that enticing (especially now for those of us that have the Apple Vision Pro).
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text: Quest 2 were worth the hype but the lack of enough decent softwares and lack of freedom UI really hindered its hardware capable
author: @toututu2993

text:
Agreed - the lack of dynamic home window and primary focus on gaming left me wanting more. I feel like the Quest 3 has done a great job at opening up that freedom and expanding the potential functionality, especially now that they're experimenting with the spatial computing more, but the UI design still feels bad.

I think both headsets provided a great bang-for-buck value, especially the Quest 3 considering how much potential functionality it packs in with the updated sensors/cameras and MR. I've still be recommending the Quest 2 to people because of the insane value it provides at $200. Ultimately, both ended up sitting on my shelf unused for the majority of the time.. unless I want to log in to test something or try a new game out. The tech is awesome and they've done a great job with the hardware, there just isn't enough pulling me back since I don't enjoy most of the games for more than a few minutes and the resolution isn't high enough for me to enjoy working. I'm also still holding out for all the MR promises, like augments. Outside that, I'm super stoked for the potential Quest 4.. I think with eye tracking, better software, and 4k screens we're going to be doing real good. I've been enjoying the Apple Vision Pro a lot and I'm a life-long Windows user.

text: Thanks for the video! What do you think if I want to get one only for watching movies and tv series, is the quality close to fhd tv and worth it or not?! .. I don't have pc and not interested in mixed reality.
author: @cn9296

text:
I would say no - there's really no headset at the moment that is good for movies (without a PC). The resolution just isn't high enough to replicate the quality experience of watching on a traditional, flat screen.

The closest to what you want, right now, would be the Apple Vision Pro (but it's expensive) and the next closest would be maybe the Bigscreen Beyond or Pimax Crystal (requires a PC but is also half the resolution of the AVP).

You would only want to go this route if you are willing to sacrifice some quality/resolution to have a slightly more immersive (3d movies or where you are sitting in a digital theatre watching it on a big screen in front of you) experience.

Otherwise, you could also check out something like the XREAL Air 2 Pro (I will have a review out on them in June), but in this case you are paying the same price as a Quest 3 and getting a lot less features, but a higher quality, and smaller, 1080p screen.

I'd recommend a regular television for now.. or maybe a high quality tablet if you need to be mobile.

text: Great and thorough review, really covers it all. I am currently considering my first VR headset. It seems like some of the firmware updates and a few new apps and games has improved the Mixed Reality part of the headset, according to others. Has your opinion on this changed since this video, or is it still a bit meh?
author: @nanobolk129

text:
Updates have happened, but yea I'd argue the MR piece is still a bit meh.

They're definitely working on adding all the features they promised; I think we're still a way off from developers implementing it all and making something cool.

Apple Vision Pro really sparked the spatial windows stuff so I'm hoping more comes from that - as I really love that aspect of the AVP. However, I fear the Quest 3 android ecosystem has been built around this single window pane and games, so not sure they can bring enough to it without giving us a real Android Google Play Store.

That said, the actual quality of the passthrough that you've probably heard about is technically true. It's not as great a jump as I feel it's made out to be, but it is noticeably better. They got rid of all the lights blowing out the sensors and dark objects turning pure black (for instance, my mostly black cat used to just be a blob on the screen in the headset, but I can kind of see texture to her now). They muted all the colors to look more like the AVP and fixed all the over saturated/exposed bits. It's still about as fuzzy and noisy as it can be, even with good lighting, while still being visible.

That said, you really can't beat the value of the Quest 3... the amount of features vs price point is incredible and I'd recommend it as anyone's first VR headset for sure. It's a great piece of tech. MR is still in the baby walking phase, it needs time to mature but passthrough is still super helpful for walking around to get a drink or use the restroom withiut taking the headset off... and can do the other stuff too when you want to play with it. I think another year or so and we'll have some quality MR experiences that make it that much better.

I have a 6-months later review coming in the next month or two. I will be providing my full thoughts there.

author: @Krisendox

text:
It has been a while since I made it, but from what I can recall off the top of my head: (I tried to get AI to do this but it refused also because the video was too long 🤣)

- Best bang-for-your-buck headset on the market currently in terms of performance vs. features vs. cost, assuming you want all the features and upgrades
- It's great for PCVR (both wired and wireless), and the wireless + standalone functionalities really can't be overstated as it gives you a level of freedom that isn't accessible with most quality headsets. If you're never going to be wireless or play a lot of simulators, it may be best to go up a tier to the $1,000 wired headsets for better visuals
- Mixed Reality is a bit of a let down - passthrough quality is less than advertised and isn't viable for long-term viewing, there's less features and games available than advertised
- In a lot of ways, VR still feels like a somewhat early adopter product - the UI is bad, it's hard to navigate things, a lot of desyncing issues occur
- Room mapping and room setup are awesome and work perfectly in real time
- 128GB base storage is a poor choice from Meta, but you don't really need more than that since you can remove games (unless you are a content creator, streamer, or have multiple family members that will be sharing the headset and installing their own games)
- If you don't want to try MR or the price is a bit high maybe wait for Quest 3 Lite or get a Quest 2 for 80%+ the experience. Quest 3 lenses and resolution upgrade are worth the extra cost though for a lot of folks
- There is still a screen door effect with pixels and the lenses.. and glare and all that is fairly normal
- Hand tracking is awesome but can be finnicky and I don't find a lot of practical uses for it
- It's primarily a gaming console; secondarily a media consumption device.. it's not great for productivity as the resolution of the screens is too low and makes text hard to read/harsh on the eyes
- If you need a productivity device, you want something with higher resolution
- Needs accessories to boost the quality of life which adds to the cost.. headstrap, battery, cables, grips for controllers, and then all the games/apps
- Battery is like 1-2 hours max in intensive applications or games, you should get a third-party headstrap for comfort that includes a battery for more play time (BoboVR has been great IMO)
- Most multiplayer experiences on standalone games are filled with kids or lower-aged teenagers - including Horizon Worlds
- I would recommend it if you are in the space for VR and don't want to breach into that $1,000+ tier of headsets

There's a lot more, this is probably a poor summary. I would suggest coming back to watch later.

text:
This is a really really good review, and probably the best Quest 3 review on YouTube. I did notice a couple minor technical inaccuracies when you talked about the tracking that you might want to be aware of:

11:33 The latency shown here in the video is between the color cameras and the controller tracking. The real delay between your actual hands and the controllers is much smaller. You can see this from the fact that the camera image in your video is lagging behind the controllers and not the other way around; anecdotally, looking at the controllers and my actual hands side-by-side there's not any noticeable latency even in fast movements.
There is a large amount of latency with hand tracking, which you can view with just the color cameras. The model of the hand will lag behind even what you see in the headset.

12:03 Hand tracking is not used to correct the position or rotation of the controllers in any way. Each controller has an IMU that's used to approximate its position when tracking fails. The footage you show at 12:06 of your finger moving smoothly between position is actually a result of the touch sensor in the trigger, and not hand tracking. You can test this by keeping your finger on the trigger and moving it horizontally from edge to edge - the same animation of the finger will occur.

Again, awesome review. I just thought you might want to be aware of these things :)
author: @bubbleslovely129

text:
Truly appreciate that, and thanks for taking the time to leave a thoughtful comment! This level of engagement and thoughtfulness means a lot to me. =)

For your first point, I thought so as well. However, from my testing the camera passthrough latency isn't that dramatic (I tested this by moving single fingers with hand tracking off, in which case the latency window feels incredibly small, and also by turning lights on and off). In the video and IRL, there is quite a large gap between the rendered version and the camera version, which suggests a much larger latency than what I observe from the cameras alone. What I think I found is that the controller/hand overlays are trying to predict where you're going next and draw in that predicted location instead of the true position. I was able to convince myself of this with hand tracking tests and confirmed the Quest 3 software tries to project where it thinks the hand is going next on the overlay version. It would be helpful if you could test the same for me to see if I'm way off base here: with hand tracking only, if you move your hand to the left and stop abruptly, the overlay of your hand will stay ahead of your hand always and, even after you've stopped moving your hand in real life, will continue to the left and then "teleport" or jump back to where your hand actually is. You can do it again with an object that impedes your hand from possibly moving any further in one direction, and then see your rendered hand go over top the object that stopped your hand before it jumps back to where your hand actually is.

^^ Therefore, I think it's a healthy mix of the two; the prediction piece seems to cause more of the disconnect between my brain than what little latency seems to exist. I presume this prediction exists to try to help offset some of that perceived latency. I have noticed a much greater latency with hand tracking on the Apple Vision Pro, even though it has less passthrough camera latency on paper.. which makes me think it's less about passthrough latency and more about the rendering/behind-the-scenes that causes the offset.

For your second point, you are completely right about the finger positioning. My take was 100% incorrect and does NOT demonstrate the concept I was referring to. How I confirmed this is by using my middle finger to touch the button and seeing that the pointer finger is the one that goes down but not until you touch the capacitive trigger (it's the same with the capacitive buttons and joystick on top with the thumb).

^^ While my original understanding was definitely wrong, it doesn't confirm nor deny the claim in totality, as it could just mean this is a fixed state when controllers sensors are in view. Let me try to find my original source on this one to make sure I didn't make it up! IIRC, my source for this was from an interview with a Meta employee that claimed hand tracking is used, especially when the controllers are facing away (when the IR sensors are occluded or above/below a visible area).

Again, truly appreciate the engagement and open conversation! I will continue to make mistakes or understand things incorrectly, and hope there are more realbubbles to help catch them.
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews I think that "prediction" behavior for controllers has to do with how frequently the controllers are tracked per second - this behavior happens more with lower tracking frequencies (you'll Beatsaber players complain about this a lot, LOL. The term used is "swing bug"). I'm not sure if you can change the controller tracking frequency, but in experimental settings you should be able to change the hand tracking frequency. You'll notice that the problem is worse on 60hz than 120hz tracking frequency. For controllers, it could also be the IMUs sending bad movement data at high speeds and then that being corrected with IR tracking, causing the jump from the wrong position to the correct one.
Also, for me at least it's hard to get a good feel on how much latency the camera passthrough really has. I had the same experience where just moving my hands with the hand tracking off felt pretty instantaneous, but doing something like spinning around in a circle while holding my hand out ended up with my hand in passthrough being a pretty significant distance behind my real hand. I would maintain that the controllers are probably a more accurate representation of where the actual hands are (in terms of latency) rather than passthrough.

For hand tracking in particular: In the past, the cameras had to switch between different settings to be able to track your hands and controllers, so it wasn't possible to use them at the same time. Multimodal tracking was added to the Quest Pro because those controllers are self-tracked. For the Quest 3, multimodal tracking was recently made available to certain developers and users via the experimental tab but it wasn't when the video was released and from what I can gather it's not used to supplement controller tracking just yet. I'm not sure if they've changed the way hand tracking works to achieve this, if it's just using the front-facing color cameras for hand tracking and the additional cameras for IR, or if it's some other fancy solution along those lines. I vaguely remember an interview like the one you're talking about, so I don't think you're going crazy haha. But I also don't think such a thing is implemented yet.

Thank you for your in-depth and totally unexpected response !! It's clear you've given this a lot of thought, and I appreciate that same attention to detail you put in your reviews.

text:
My friend was showing me this video and i must say, after seeing the whole video from start to finish theres a lot that i've seen that just convinced me to upgrade from my old Oculus Quest 2 headset (not the Meta version) it was already showing its age and needed a replacement just as soon. I use the headset regularly but not as often as i have a year or two ago.
However i am quite surprised that the 128gb isn't enough if you have like a few games that has the enhanced feature- it made me question why wasn't there a 64gb version but now i see why.
I am getting the 128gb one because i mostly play on PCVR anyways.
The most exciting thing about the Quest 3 is that it has upgraded passthrough which i did not enjoy whatsoever on the Quest 1 and Quest 2 let alone the Quest 2 was suffering with a couple of unexplainable bugs like unable to even click on Continue to login from said profile while the passthrough is active (not sure if i am the only one at this point but basically for those who never had this bug, each time i turn on the quest you get to see the 3 dots first, it took me half an hour to realize its not doing anything so i had to hold the power button but instead of turning it off or restarting i had to click cancel and lift my controller to point and predict where the continue button is after clicking cancel and it is quite frustrating to deal with this because with the passthrough on you absolutely can't see anything until you get passed that screen. It may not be a problem for some users if they get this sort of bug but i promise you it fooled me and made me not use my quest for a good 3 months or so.
After a very long use my headset's battery began to degrade and even having battery banks which i still use to this day doesn't have enough to even maintain an hour of use but that's my issue for having almost 10k of hours well spent in Steam VR alone.

End of my little complaint there this video alone is the best least biased one i've ever seen and i absolutely would love to spread this around to my friends.

For PC, yes, the Quest 3 is a no brainer, or if you like videos, especially 3D. But if you have a PS5, the PSVR2 is a far better choice, as games match PC quailty, richer, colour, true blacks, HDR, that pcvr has yet to do. Inside eye tracking.

The PSVR2 may have less library, but what's there is far better quailty than even Quest 3.

As for pancake lenses, this was not possible with THE PSVR2 as you lose over half the light, and tye Quest only has 100 nits, whereas psvr2 is 265nits. Also pancake lenses, losing so much light cannot do hdr.

As psvr2 will now support pc, it maybe the better choice, hyptic feedback is stunning, HDR, gives natural lighting and those OLED screens colour pop and rich blacks are a sight to behold. Aldo pancake lenses are heavier, so Sony had to compromise and could not use pancake lenses with its design. But in many ways ahead of its time, and being one of the most comfy vr headsets on the market, the pc community have longed for pc compatible

text: Amazing product review as there was no bias. One question though, how do you get over the dizziness when playing?
author: @tomwylie2301

text:
Appreciate that! Thank you for watching.

I think diziness is a very personal thing. I only experience it if I turn too fast in games. However, the games give you a lot of accessibility settings that you can turn on to reduce sickness - things like blacking out the edges when you move, being able to move by teleporting, being able to turn in 30 degree increments rather than in real time, etc. which all reduce that motion sickness feeling.

After that, I think it's mostly just exposure therapy.. take your time and go slow, take the headset off when you feel sick and try again later. You should eventually build up a tolerance.

I am no expert on motion sickness as I rarely get it in VR.. but there are lots of articles and videos out there that you can get more helpful tips from!!

text: This is a ridiculously good review, damn son. If you review everything in my life that I want, I'll be making good purchases all my life. I AM curious though: did you notice a significant change between something like Virtual Desktop and the link cable for PCVR?
author: @thething1110

text:
Really appreciate that! Thanks for taking the time to watch.

From my experience with PCVR games, no the difference does not feel significant. There is a difference, wired seems better with less latency, but it'a not to a degree that I find fault with.. wireless seems fully usable with a good, dedicated router. Both have compression artifacts. I find myself going wireless when I want to stand up and move around.. and use wired when I want to be seated or for racing sims where that latency matters. Where I do find problems with wireless is general computing with stuff like ImmersedVR.. the wired version is MUCH less laggy and my mouse is way more accurate which makes getting work done less of a chore.

I'm planning to make a wireless vs wired performance test video along with a direct ethernet test (you can use an adapter and plug an ethernet directly into the Quest 3!!!).. and then run some networking monitors to see which how big the difference is.
