text: I only have Meta 2. Understand the premise of M3 though, with the pass thru boost and mixed reality capacity. My feeling is that the Metaverse is Soooooooooooooooooo close to being an epic innovation event that'll put multiple entire Fortune 500 companies out of business. Yet it's not there yet. How can Mr. Zuckerberg become the absolute undisputed tech King of Kings? He has to improve the Battery Life + field of View + Resolution + content Library + Ergonomics / lightness of these headsets. With Meta 2 my initial experience was gasping in utter awe at the 3d immersion experience...but then like gee this is a bit heavy, clunky, annoying, on my head. Then a lot of the content on the meta store were gimmicky games or stunt like stuff. A skyscraper plank walk. A roller coaster ride while a skeleton is sitting next to you and you are being chased by a dinosaur. Virtual mini golf or table tennis. Etc. On and on like this. Compare these to robust games and apps like Fallout 4 or Call of the Sea, or Microsoft Excel or Adobe Premiere. History repeats. When cinema just started out, you had gimmicky stunts like footage of a train about to hit the audience. The initial 'nickelodeon' theaters had no George Lucas or Steven Spielberg type master storytellers yet. Audience's ate it up for a while. Metaverse MIGHT make all the paradigms for phones, TVs, and laptops we currently have now and inherited from the 20th century, feel completely stale. Like how personal computers once made IBM mainframes seem. Are companies with a heavy emphasis on big bulky awkward atom based TVs, mouses, or keyboards dead men walking? I.e. Sony, Samsung, Logitech, Vizio, Toshiba, LG, Onn, TCL. Why the hell, once these headsets are perfected, would you want to spend even a single penny on a BIG HEAVY 20th century paradigm TV, that requires a big table / stand / arm system to hold up? That limit your mobility? That takes up a lot of space in rooms? That can't follow you around to another room. Say from the living room to kitchen to patio to bathroom to bedroom. That is hard to adjust if you just change your body position and thus viewing angle. Say go from sitting to standing to lying down flat in your body position? For $200 bucks with even just the Meta 2 you can feel like you are in the front room at a movie theater with a huge wall sized TV, that can be moved around smooth as silk to exactly the spot you desire for perfect viewing. You can be sitting on the metro waiting for your train to arrive with a giant 80 inch virtual TV in front of you and with total visual immersion / privacy. Then when the train arrives, walk onto the train and sit down and reposition the "TV" in exactly the right spot visually and continue watching your content. These types of scenarios are an absolute game changer. Once people no longer buy 20th century type TVs in ten years. This will affect CONTENT companies like Disney, HBO, or Paramount massively too. They have such a psychological and substantial investment in 20th century paradigms for content creation and distribution. Can they cross the bridge into the 21st century? For some reason IBM and Xerox could not become Microsoft or Apple. History now likely repeats. "NEW wine in NEW wine skins".

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews ​I got my quest 3 yesterday and the setup was pretty good exept that you need the meta app on your phone for some reason.
Picking up the controllers made me smile with no ring, light weight and they have good ergonomics.
Wearing the Headset for the first time made me realise how damn uncomfortable it is it started hurting me after a few minutes already. Luckly i had a few accessories on its way to me.
Next up i tried Mixed reality games since it was marketed so much. Sadly for me they just feel like tech demos nothing more.
After this i tried the air link option since i was waiting on my link cable and it was pretty bad. Okay i dont have the best router but traking and image quality was bad to to pont where the picture would freeze for a moment. So i gave up for the day.

The next day i got my accessories and my link cable. Putting on the accessories instanly transformend the quest 3 into something comfortable witch you can wear easily for longer periods.
Plugging in the link cable witch also has a way to connect the charger. After setting up the link cable and starting up some games i was blown away now the quest 3 finally started to shine. With the new lenses you can read text out of the corner of your eye and you dont have to look straight at it. The sweet spot is also way bigger where everything is sharp.
Now to gaming. I loaded up beat saber and smahed evey single higescore of me in the first try, so it is save to say it works really well so i will 100% play more vr than usual. The tracking also worked really great now. I will try some driving sims in the next couple of days but i guess it will be amazing.
All in all i can say the upgrade is woth it from a rift s if you are playing a good amount of vr. Is it woth the price as a new vr user? i dont think so i would say get a quest 2 first for half the price.
Is it worth it for pc vr 100% yes its really good bang for your buck but you have to be willing to spend like 100 bucks on accessories to make it good enough for longer sessions.

text:
Hi Aline, Excellent review and sound quality is just amazing in your videos! 

Just needed your help, How is the display quality on Quest 3 ? Many people say that the display is grainy and looks like 480p or 720p?

I wanna buy Quest 3 mainly for watching movies and tv shows. Also, how is the 3d movie experience ?
author: @dhiraj1199

text:
Appreciate that, thank you for taking the time to watch!

Let me know if this breakdown helps or confuses:

The Quest 3 has a native panel resolution of 2064x2208, per eye.. which is more pixels than a 2560x1440 (1440p) computer monitor, per eye.

Most Quest 3 standalone experiences run at a max of 1680x1760 or above.. which is just over a 1080p screen, per eye. Some games/apps are already optimized to go above this; for ones that aren't, you can download apps like Quest Games Optimizer or others to try to boost this resolution, OR you can use it with a PC and supersample a high resolution stream from there to get it to the native panel resolution.

Regardless of the above, if you are watching a movie in the headset, even with a large screen in front of you, the virtual display is only taking up a portion of the Quest 3's native screens.. because there is all the other stuff around you being rendered (for example, if you're in Bigscreen watching a movie, there's the theatre living room around you, and the screen is only taking up like 60% of that environment). This technically makes the content look a bit sharper than it would taking up the whole screen, but you have to keep in mind you're now using like 60% of a 1080p resolution to watch the content.

On top of that, the PPD is low enough to where the lenses magnify the screen and you can see individual pixels and even the gaps between them.. giving a bit of screen door effect. Plus, there's extra distortion from all the lights and brightness emitting from the screen.

In other words, a lot of the times it feels like you're watching 720p content on a 1080p screen and that description is fairly accurate.

It can't compete with a traditional TV.. not even a 2k monitor , for watching content. It's also not OLED so blacks don't get super black.

If you use Quest Games Optimizer, however, you can bump your apps resolution up to like 150-200%, and then make the screen as large as possible, you can get a decent experience. Again, it's not going to replace your TV, but it is watchable and I've heard a lot of people using it.

For me, I only use it to watch YouTube videos here and there. I tried a movie once, but I'd rather watch it on my TV. The coolness factor is being able to watch it in a virtual space. I don't find the resolution to be high enough to pull me back to watching videos. Once you start watching, you don't really notice the pixels and glare anymore as you get immersed in the movie. It's a great alternative if you don't have a TV in your room and don't want to watch movies on your phone. Outside of that, it's more a secondary fun experience - and you're gonna have a better visual experience watching from a TV or computer screen.

3D experiences work - I've only tried Avatar: The Way of Water in 3D and it worked well. Generally, the effects felt somewhat subtle but it was nice to have. It was a 1080p blueray that felt like I was watching it in 720p with the compression and distortion. I can't find higher resolution 3D movies so that seems like the problem there.
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews Wow, thank you so much for sharing such a detailed explanation. This definitely helps me to keep realistic expectations from Quest 3.

And yes, it's a little deceiving (but not intentionally). The recordings you see are a screen capture directly on the device from 1 of the 2 screens. In other words, it is not what the user is seeing directly, because they are looking at that through magnifying lenses with a bunch of flaws. Therefore, the screen recordings look much more crisp and perfect than what is seen through the headset.

Maybe a good example is think of taking a screen recording of your screen directly.. if you play that back, it will look crisp and perfect. Now, record your screen with your phone and play that back.. it's much worse and lower resolution. The phone is a representation of your eyes in the headset (but in the headset its even worse because of the lenses and distance from your eyes).

text:
Hi Aline, i have a question. I like watching movies and series. I also like to play casual games.
How is the image quality compared to a miniled or oled tablet, when watching movies? As ive never tried a vr before, i have a hard time imagining it.

Also, as for games, are we capable of downloading these games from the web and using the VR as a “computer”? Think either genshin impact or games that doesnt have high requirements.
It would be nice if i could just lay in bed with the vr headset and chill out for an hour or two on occasion.
author: @Aneliuse

text:
Hey there!

The image quality is quite a bit less than an OLED tablet. You could try imagining an LCD screen, really up close to your face, and zoomed in like 5-8x (and with diagonal pixel gaps). You can see the diagonal pattern of pixels while watching content and, on top of that, you get quite a bit of "lens glare" and "mura" and an extra layer of particles from the lenses. It's a fairly decent "screen door effect".

I will say that it mostly goes unnoticed by me once I'm engaged and immersed in content - especially games. During movies longer than YouTube videos, though, I find that the glare in the lenses (especially from dark scenes with bright light - think a black background with white text) really detracts from the experience as it highlights that dirty lens effect.

For games, if it's a PC game then yes you can load it on your PC and stream the gameplay to your headset! You can do this a flat or curved screen using an app like Virtual Desktop... and can make the screen as virtually large as you want (which is neat). You can also load some Android apps directly, but you can't get them directly from the Google Play store - you must "sideload" them via your computer. Further, Xbox and others also have streaming services. So, yes, perfect use case for the Quest 3!

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.

text:
1. Does it feel motion sickness?
2. If i want to play game on quest 3 and i dont want 360 view. I want just a small monitor. Is it possible to do that with every game?
author: @aforashish7

text:
1. This is subjective and very dependent on each person; if you get motion sick easy IRL, then you will probably get motion sick in VR. It is a common phenomenon and most games provide some settings (vignetting the edges to reduce motion blur and teleporting or snapping turn movement rather than real-time movement) to reduce motion sickness. I did not experience it in most cases, but in some fast-paced games with the settings turned off, turning too fast can make me feel nauseous while I'm moving.

2) No, not every game. Most VR games cannot be played like you describe: games like Assassin's Creed Nexus, Population One, etc. all rely on the VR hand movements and controls so you cannot play them on a "flat" screen. However, if you have Xbox games (Forza Horizon 5, God of War, etc.) or PC games (Apex Legends, DOTA, etc.), you can play those with a virtual monitor - just keep in mind there will be minor input lag.

text:
Good review, I've had mine for a week and agree with all of this.

I got the BOBOVR M3 head strap and it was a huge improvement. Maybe because I have a rather large head it was very uncomfortable and was always coming loose with the included strap.

This is my third VR HMD. I got it in part because of the wireless aspect of it, and it is great to play Beat Saber or any of the games that you can load on it wirelessly.

I haven't had the same luck with PCVR when I've tried to play PCVR wirelessly with Quest Link wireless and Virtual desktop. Even with a fairly new router it stutters and sound cuts out. Not sure if I need a dedicated router. I did try it with a cable (Quest Link wired) with Half Life Alyx and it worked great. The most fun I've had so far was Beat Saber and a basic flight combat sim called Warplanes Battle over the Pacific.

It's disappointing there aren't more MR apps. It would be cool to have a game where zombies invaded your house, etc.
author: @zombygunslinger

text:
Appreciate that!

I also had the BoboVR M3 head strap and agree it is a massive improvement in balance and face pressure. They released a new S3 version with an upgraded battery (larger, but also faster charger capability for the Quest 3) - you can get a discount if you bought the M3 recently. I have a video in the works testing 7 different battery solutions and these 2 are part of that test.. I hope to have that out early April.

Wireless capability, IMO, is still the main value proposition of this headset. I think I'm heavily into the idea of Pimax Crystal/Varjo Aero/Bigscreen Beyond in terms of visuals and PCVR use, but the wireless capability of the Quest 3 is just so convenient and doesn't give me that claustrophobic feeling of being tethered.

I'm sorry to hear you're having issues with wireless PCVR - I would check the settings and proximity. I had a similar issue, I have WAP's mounted to my ceilings upstairs and downstairs and I had a bit of lag on them as my phones, smart home devices, etc. are attached to them and the closest one is 10 feet away from my PC setup. I bought a refurbished $60 router on Amazon to connect to my PC for direct/dedicated wifi and it has been working extremely well.. so well it's difficult for me to tell there's much latency.

Feels like we have a fairly similar experience with the headset.. I'm also waiting on more MR drops. I feel they really hyped this feature up and just had absolutely nothing to show for it on launch. I can see they're slowly incrementing on the capabilities but feels like it should have been saved for a separate device.

text: Subbed, awesome review! Curious if this review holds up to the latest MQ3 updates? I scooped one up and im excited to play around with it soon. I have worked on hand tracking a few years ago with the Leap Motion which was really neat. Recently for work I have been experimenting with an Apple Vision Pro and learning the development pipeline for it and have been really impressed with its hand tracking... but not the cost of getting my own for personal dev tinkering lol So I spent some time looking at other options and landed on a Quest 3, I plan to noodle around with hand tracking and make something that I could maybe eventually take to AVP. I really like the idea of no controllers (if the experience allows for it) If you have any recommendations for hand tracking focused experiences I'd be interested in hearing about those as well :)
author: @MrNsanders90

text:
Truly appreciate that!

I would say the majority of my review still holds up. They've made a handful of good improvements like adding 120hz to Quest Link's PCVR, letting you save and name spaces in your house (there was an issue where it would fail to remember you spaces), software improvements to the microphone quality (untested), and so on.. but nothing that has flipped anything I said in this review to the opposite side. IMO, the coolest updates are that developers can now implement upper body tracking to track your hands/wrists/arms/shoulders/torso and occlusion in mixed reality (which is awesome because your furniture can block objects that are on the other side of them).. however, both require developers to implement them and there's very few experiences outside the Meta demos at this point. Overall, I find there's still very limited uses of mixed reality, inside out body tracking, and hand tracking.

Hopefully you as a developer can help push that forward. =)

I find hand track ng to be awesome and frustrating at the same time.
When it works (which is often for basic things like pointing, gesturing to click, opening menus, or even physically pressing buttons), it's awesome. It's very finnicky in my experience - often not triggering my gestures properly or firing triggers that I didn't intentionally make. It also still has a lot of trouble detecting top-down finger movement like using a virtual keyboard, so you have to type with pointer fingers. It feels a bit wonky to me still; the best way I can describe it is it works 75% of the time unless you go extremely slow and are very intentional with all your movements.. but that defeats the purpose of it being second nature. I love it because I don't like to play a lot of games and the controllers feel cumbersome, but I still find myself reaching for the controllers a lot because I get frustrated.

Anecdotally, hand tracking also seems to drain the battery significantly quicker... it's very noticeable, especially in mixed reality, which is why I'm mentioning it.

Definitely check out "Cubism" - its a 3D puzzle game that lets you drag and move pieces around with your hands to fit them together. I don't have much else, unfortunately. I've seen that "Hand Physics Lab" exists, but I haven't tried it yet - it's specifically built for hand tracking as the name suggests. I tried a HANDful of other experiences that suggested they were made for hand tracking + mixed reality, but they fail to work as intended.

I would love to hear what you find as you explore as I am also on the hunt for more use cases of hand tracking. I truly prefer it to holding onto controllers.

text: I've watched a pile of reviews on the Q3 and this is by far the best, great job! I'm looking to upgrade from my Rift S and the Q3 seems pretty cool. I saw that you can increase the resolution in the headset and in fact, by default, it doesn't run at its native resolution? Have you tried increasing this? I'm not sure if this was only when using it with a PC or in general though.
author: @davefellows

text: Ah, just saw you mention this (hadn't finished watching when I commented :))
author: @davefellows

text:
Appreciate that, thanks for watching!

In standalone, by default, I believe apps run at 168x1760 - which is like 20% lower than the screen's resolution. App developers can, of course, go above this, but hard to say that many do based on the apps I've used (and a lot of games are still on the Quest 2 resolution which was even lower). You can use a third-party app called "Quest Games Optimizer" which is only $10 and allows you to scale up to the native screen resolution (or even oversample) which seems to do the trick at the cost of increased battery usage (in other words, unusable with default battery but very comfortable if you get one of the 10,000mAh battery packs). It allows you to control other things like FPS, individual rendering qualities, etc.

For PCVR, all those settings are built into the launcher or game you use - Virtual Desktop has been my go-to and lets you select video render resolutions.

text: I would like to point out that the latency you noted with the controller tracking while in pass through isn't actually latency of the controller tracking catching up with your movements. Look closely and you'll see the virtual controllers/hand outlines move first as you move them, and then your "real" hands catch up. It threw me for a loop as well, but the controller tracking appears to be on a slightly lower latency path than the color pass through cameras, which is what you want and why the controllers feel so smooth when immersed.
author: @NaraukoFNH

text:
I ended up testing this a lot, and I believe the passthrough latency isn't that high. I did a lot of tests (and described those in depth on another comment from recently) but the latency of passthrough simply isn't large enough to create the visible gap seen in the video.

What I've found is it seems the controllers are "predicting" where they are going next based on your movement, and doing so quite aggressively. That paired with the latency, makes the effect super exaggerated as seen in the video (the video probably adds additional lag as it was recording at the same time).

You can test this without controllers as well by using your hands.. the 3D hand model is trying to predict your next location. You can confirm by using a wall, your other arm, or some object in front of you. Make this object so that it blocks/stops the pathway of your hand. Move your hand so that it hits the object (and stops). You will see that the render of your hands or controllers will keep going past the object, then stop and jump, or snap, back to where your hand actually is.

All that to say, I do this it is partially passthrough latency, but the larger factor from my perspective seems to be this "prediction" algorithm that's trying to guess where you're going next to compensate for whatever additional lag is happening behind the scenes with rendering the controller/hand position on top of the passthrough feed.

author: @codyschwoch5451

text:
I understood the first half of this, but in the second half you kind of fell off into a garbled mess that I presume must have been autocorrect that went sideways.

The concern of this device watching you is extremely legitimate and does not fit into the paranoid nutjob category. Meta makes its money from user data to sell ads. It is their primary source of revenue. There's a primary reason they have made Raybans with cameras in them (and that reason is not so you can make fun tik toks). They want to see everything you see and everything inside your home so they can make correlations for future products. It's ignorant to think otherwise, not paranoid to think of it. This data is going into AI so they can learn to better leverage ads and sell products - it's the only way they can continue to grow.

The other concern is similar to IP cameras and robot vacuums with cameras - the have the same data privacy issue, but also run the risk of being maliciously exploited by third parties due to lapses in software.

But yes, everyone buying should be aware of that at the time of purchase and not purchase otherwise.

Neither of these outcomes is overly paranoid, they are legitimate security concerns.

author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
​@@aline.tech.reviews Well sorry for sounding harsh there but i think there's some contradiction when it comes to mixed reality and menus.
Having everything be optional is a much better idea. Why not have both menus and posters or "portals to another world"? The tech isn't quite, you're just wearing some glasses that still have menus like you're scrolling on a phone but without the phone. That's not revolutionary and you say this when it comes to instagram so it's slightly contradictory but not in a direct way.
The tech isn't there yet for sure, menus are faster, you are right and you do want everything to be more productive but imagine that 90% of humanity adopts this and the headset becomes lightweight unintrusive glasses. Imagine that you go to the cinema (not that you need to) and instead of a still picture you're seeing a moving picture like in harry potter.
It's not about simplicity or ease of use, it's about THE MAGIC and magic sells it to people.
Why not have your room be decorated in virtual reality? It's also about control, about having options and choices, personalization and visual appeal. I can see the potential and menus aren't appealing to me in that aspect. It doesn't sell the idea of "virtual reality" or "mixed reality". This current piece of tech is very limited so, to me, it's more like a marketing strategy, an investment for the future of spacial computing and not about the product itself which won't last anyway.
Again, the tech is not quite there yet because you would need a synchcronization system so that you can see through your VR what other people are doing in real time (like a "VR internet") so atm menus are a must while magical posters that only make it harder to do what you want should be optional but... we will get there.
I can see downsides tho. A worlwide virtual reality riddled with ads wherever you look so, the magical potential becomes more intrusive marketing. You mention all of this except that I can see why they're pushing the MR into it.

text:
I'm not sure it's a lie as much as different perspectives, potentially nose shape, and not wearing the headset tight enough.

I don't get a ton of light bleed from there - I can see through the nose area if I angle my eyes purposefully down, but I also use that tiny hole as a marker for my position in the room when I'm fully in VR. It's at the bottom and doesn't let light bleed into the headset (in other words, there is no difference of experience inside the headset for me if I completely cover that small hole vs. not when the screens are on).

Light bleed can exist from there if I don't tighten the headset properly on my head - but that seems like user error.

Yea, I've got like 5-6 battery solutions that I either bought or were sent to me from companies as a result of this video. I'm currently testing them all to make a batter comparison video. I hope to have that finished by early March! I will do a comparison of idle + play + charging times and all that.

text: I was thinking about purchasing a Quest 3 instead of Vision Pro for my productivity and work related stuff but I'm skeptical if I should. I use my PC for work and wear glasses. How viable would Q3 be? I need 2-3 monitors to work and not sure if programs on my PC would be mirrored to the headset.
author: @theWardennn

text:
Your entire monitors can be displayed in the headset (not just apps), and you can add additional (up to 5?) virtual displays in apps like ImmersedVR. With the default Quest Link app or Virtual Desktop app, you are limited to 1 giant monitor at a time, but can toggle through your physical displays (no virtual displays).

Whether or not it's viable is up to your work schedule and whether you use a laptop. It's definitely a fun and interesting experience, and I really enjoy the concept. However, as someone that manages projects and does a lot of reading, writing, and coding, I find that text is difficult to read due to the diagonal pixels, gaps between, and low resolution. It's a fun, novel experience to hop into for 2-3 hours here or there, but really that's all I can take before my eyes begin to hurt. I had hoped I could use it to work in all day, but I really can't due to that level of discomfort.

If you plan to do full work sprints like 8+ hours, I wouldn't recommend it. If you only need it for 2-3 hour sprints here and there, you can probably manage. If you use a laptop for work, it's probably a good idea to extend your screens on the go and completely wirelessly.

In other words, if this is your primary use case then I would skip it.. and if you have other uses for the Quest 3 (playing games, watching movies, etc.) then it's worth it.

For me, it's not good enough yet for a full productivity monitor replacement. I'd suggest waiting for the Immersed Visor - 4k micro OLED which is 2x the resolution increase over the Quest 3 for only a small price increase.

text:
Appreciate that, and agreed on the AR/MR shortcomings.

I'm not sure I'd say there's much better on the market, period, unless one needs to niche down into a very specific use case. For example, I have been heavily considering the Pimax Crystal which should be better for sim racing, but it's also 3x the price, lacks wireless capability (for now), is massive, and the software/user experience isn't quite there. It's hard to justify upgrading into any of those niches since the Quest 3 can handle them with like 80% of the specialty (whether that be clarity, latency, OLED screens, or what-have-you).

I'm finding myself having trouble pulling the trigger on either the Bigscreen Beyond + Pimax Crystal + Varjo Aero because the Quest 3 can do anything they can and each device has its own perfections and flaws.

I do notice quite a bit of compression artifacts in PCVR on the Quest 3 but I probably haven't dialed in my settings enough. I find it frustrating to set up sim racing games like Assetto Corsa

author: @Sepguru

text:
I appreciate that, thanks for watching!

In my case, that has certainly been true. It's great fun, but it has been on the stand the majority of the time since I dropped this review. I focus most of my free time on working on my businesses or planning around them.. and there's nothing really "pulling me back" to the Quest 3 (except making more reviews - I am working on on comparing battery straps now and that's been my primary use of the device).

I've found there isn't an experience that has a "wow factor" enough to replace something I already have. VR games are awesome, but they're so shallow and multiplayer UNfriendly that it's hard for me to get deep. I'd rather spin up Apex Legends on my PC than any VR games on most days. I think if you enjoy single player games it will be up your alley.. mostly because of HL:Alyx, Asgard's Wrath 2, and Assassin's Creed (once you finish those it feels like you're going to be in a content lull until something else is released).

If the screen resolutions were higher, I'd definitely use it regularly for work and media consumption.. but it's just bad enough that watching it on my computer screen is still a better experience (when you factor in having to walk over, put the headset on + adjust it just right, set a boundary, load up an app, find your media, and then play). It's also much harder to multitask (watching a YouTube video while browsing the web or getting work done) unless you connect to a PC (which defeats the purpose if you're already sitting at one).

text: I want to know about audio. Does head tracking externalize binaural audio by means of head tracking? Can I produce my own binaural recordings and play them with head tracking?
author: @geickmei

text:
I may need you to clarify the question - but no, playing an audio file directly on the device would just play in the left/right channels like you had programmed it (no different than your phone / computer) and not change based on your head movement.

Spatial or positional audio is supported, though! You could build an experience (or maybe search for VR audio apps to see if any already exist you can import your audio into), then you could place an audio file into the world and move/turn your head to hear it differently out of each channel.. or have that audio spin around the user's head while their head is being tracked.

TLDR; you can't do that by default on the device with an audio player, but spatial/head tracking audio is possible if you build an experience for it. There are apps that allow you to quickly build your own game and would allow you to place audio files and attach them to the head tracking (I believe).

text:
I truly appreciate that, and most definitely!

You can check out most of my physical gear list in the description of the video. This list doesn't include my newest secondary camera and all my updated lights, but the main filming rig I used for the video is there.

I use Davinci Resolve Studio for editing. Editing is tedious and very time consuming and is the current bottleneck - it will be the first thing I outsource when I can afford it.

I have been filming in 4k resolution; it requires a lot of disk space when filming 2+ hours of talking head (all the mistakes, reshoots, etc.) and 2+ hours of b-roll footage. I work on a 2TB SSD and once completed I roll the finished projects over to an Asustor NAS that has 16TB of redundancy and stores everything on the cloud as a second backup.

I am working my way through my latest review videos; once those are completed I'm going to do a tour of the studio I've set up for filming and my main work desk where I am going to start filming secondary content.

author: @nostalgiaholic4874

text:
😆 . It's partially a dramatic re-enactment, but VR enables a lot of cool benefits.

You can use it to spawn 5 virtual displays and place them wherever you want around you. You can make them as large or as small as you want, as close or as far away as you want, customize the resolution / aspect ratio, etc. The benefit of this is that if you only have 1 monitor at your desk, or say a laptop with only 1 screen, you can expand that to 5 screens without need to buy 4 more screens and mount them.

It's also extremely portable, if I wanted to work while standing at my kitchen counter, I now can if a laptop or keyboard is over there... or if I wanted to go to a coffee shop and still benefit from 5 screens, now I can.

It's beneficial at a stationary desk like shown here as it allows me to place screens above and below me - which are impossible IRL without significant building investments. It makes glancing at information and using your main screens practical. It's also comfortable/healthy as it lets you change up your neck movements.

It's also fun because you can be anywhere you want.. in a space station orbiting Earth, looking out a window to a meadow with a lake, etc. IRL, if I turn my head all I see is walls.

There are other people there, too, that you can chat to and interact with. For instance, you can draw on whiteboards together, share screens in digital space, etc.

All around, awesome experience and one I highly recommend. I find myself in there about 2-4 hours a day and the rest I spend "looking at my computer with my own eyes".

text: Hey, are there any ways to charge your headset in oculus link? I know it should supposedly do that, but it doesnt on my quest 2 and im scared that when i eventually buy a quest 3, i’ll have to live with a higher-spec battery draining simulation device when im playing half life alyx or something. is there any “double cable” type link cable?
author: @sodicious

text:
Yes! For starters, if you have multiple USB-C's on your computer, I would try them all.. some ports on motherboards will output data only and others will provide juice.

If you don't have any, you can buy an adapter. There are 2 types you can find on Amazon: (1) a "10gbps USB C hub splitter with 100w power delivery" - which is a USB hub with 5 USB slots and one to insert power into to charge your devices that are also taking data, and (2) a "charging port link cable" which is exactly the same as a normal Quest Link cable but allows you to plug in a power source to it to charge the headset. I went with the first type because you can use it for so many other things outside the quest and it's nearly the same price as the dedicated link cable.

text: Bro is really trying hard to kills his headset with the 65W charger 💀
author: @sennahoj6741

text:
💀. Fun fact: the way devices charge is they pull only the power they need from the source (charger). The charger doesn't push 65w to the Quest 3, rather the Quest 3 simply takes the 18-27w it needs at the time.

The charger can supply up tp 65w in this caae, but it only ever supplies what the Quest 3 is asking for - which maxes out at around 27w.

Voltage is a different story, I think too high a voltage could fry your device but most chargers are in a safe range.

text: Don’t these things destroy the original battery for the device overtime? And charging while playing doesn’t that hurt the battery?
author: @trevor5672

text:
It's likely safe to assume that charging a battery, while also making heavy use of it, is going to cause the battery to degrade faster than it would otherwise. I am not an expert, but I believe that is due to high levels of heat and strain. The Quest 3 is smart enough to charge at a reduced rate while the headset is in use - likely to prevent overheating and manage the battery's state better.

That said, you're adding additional battery life life to it externally - which keeps you juiced for longer, even if the internal battery is degrading at a more quick pace than it would otherwise. I would guess this use case will still outlast the Quest 4 for most people.. especially considering you can replace your external batteries (even the internal battery doesn't seem that hard to get to if it completely died).

text: No one talks about the controllers not being aligned correctly in certain games.  In some native quest games and PCVR, the controllers aim about 15 degrees outward and there is no way to compensate for it other than to hold the controller weird.  I think the design is different than the old quest controllers and the angle is off, so games made with that angle are offset.  Also, tracking gets really bad in games like beat saber when its fast (above hard mode) or you move the controllers in a way that the headset can't see the top of it (saber starts floating across the screen).  beat saber attachments don't work whatsoever unless you play with the controllers upright at all times.  I hope they resolve this with ai and software updates sooner than later.  It completely breaks immersion.  The only resolution I have found so far if accurate tracking is a concern of yours is to buy $300 quest pro controllers with cameras on them for tracking.  It's been an issue that's been posted in their forums since at least October of this year.

author: @marcusjscofield

text:
I believe it's completely normal - I also get it, along with a good deal of blurriness around all edges. It's only at the very far edges (but all of them, up down left right). I rarely notice it in real world scenarios as I've gotten used to moving my head instead of trying to move my eyes to read text in the peripheries like that. For me it appears like at the far edge right where text/stuff gets blurry and is impossible to read anyways.

A quick Google search of Reedit shows we are not alone and the majority of users experience the same.

May be worth trying to adjust the headset a bit to see if you can get a better angle on the sweet spot.

author: @shanesanders1630

text:
Thanks for taking the time to watch! I appreciate and welcome the feedback; I don't know why I do that with my hands but will try to tone it down.

I think the appeal of virtual screens is that you would no longer be restricted to your desk. For example, if it worked I could go sit on my couch, lay on my bed, sit on the balcony, go to a coffee shop, etc. with a portable 5 screen monitor setup at high resolution. I can't do that currently, so I'm literally chair-locked to this 1 sitting area for 12+ hours a day if I wish to be productive.

Same for social scenarios - a lot of us don't want to go out to a club with beer all over the floor, slipping trying to dance, and having some random drunk guy walk over and punch you in the face for no reason. It's just not worth the experience.

Wishing for those advancements to never happen because you want humans to do "human things" is a very odd statement IMO. "Human things" will constantly evolve as we advance digitally so that feels like hanging onto a past. There will be a point you won't be able to tell a difference in touching "virtual" grass from "real" grass.. and you might already be there (in which case you are arguing against something that already exists).

Also: "VR Chat lacks a dedicated adult centric enviroment" - You sure are good at dodging bullets.
author: @serioserkanalname499

text:
I truly appreciate that! Thank you for taking the time to watch and leave a thoughtful comment.

I would say the text/virtual screens aliasing and low resolution are personally my main drawbacks with the device. My hopeful use case was to be able to work regularly on virtual screens (e.g., do my career job, plan/edit these videos, etc.). The resolution simply isn't high enough to make that worth putting on the headset over using the 3-4 displays in front of me as too much virtual real estate is needed to render the virtual world.

Gaming is definitely a fun and immersive experience, but yea - I don't spend more than a couple hours/week playing video games and I haven't had any experiences during my testing that have pulled me back outside watching YouTube videos and testing stuff. It has mostly been sitting on my desk over there untouched. I was not a fan of Assassin's Creed outside the initial climbing fun. I haven't yet tried Asgard's Wrath 2 but it's installed and waiting for me whenever I finish filming this next tablet review video.

I would say if you are on the fence it's probably a good idea to find a local shop to wear one for a bit - and if you aren't overly impressed then a Quest 2 is like 85% of the experience for less than half the price (especially if you buy used).
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
Got one for my daughter yesterday, and came away disappointed. It’s definitely better than my quest 2, but I was disappointed with the visual quality (resolution, etc). I didn’t think I’d care, but the lack of OLED was super noticeable to me. It really didn’t seem like a huge leap. The biggest plus was the overall snappiness, a huge improvement over the quest 2.

If I had bought it just for myself, I would have taken it back. Not worth $500 to me.

author: @sil3ntearth

text:
@@sil3ntearth - Agreed! I made a whole video on just how bad I felt the passthrough cameras were in comparison to how heavily marketed and hyped the clarity and functionality was. I think a lot of us were sorely disappointed there.

The majority of the price increase looks to be is coming from the new CPU/GPU and displays + lenses. A couple of supply chain analysists have posted their thoughts and the cameras only came out to like $40 and Meta is selling at a loss (subsidizing the cost of developing all the software to handle them). This is purely a guess, but I guess the Quest 3 Lite will get rid of the passthrough cameras AND either go back a generation on lenses (back to the Fresnel) or back a generation on the chipset.

text: I noticed you never compared the screen door effect to the quest 2’s, is it better or worse?
author: @christophermoore6110

text:
Yea, mostly because I don't have a Quest 2 on hand and its been a long time since I've looked through one. I would advise checking with a YouTuber that has both on hand currently for a more accurate opinion.

From what I can recall, the SDE effect seems the same or worse - the way in which the screens are rotated on the Quest 3 causes this diagonal effect that pronounces the gap between pixels - especially when trying to read text or use virtual screens (but it feels less noticeable in games).

It's one of those things where if you're playing a game or enjoying an experience, you don't notice it because it "fades into the background" and your brain ignores it. However, it's very apparent in all menus, virtual screens, and any time you're trying to read text or browse the web.

text:
Not really - it's mostly a gimmick. I think a lot of what you see that suggests you can do these things is more of a "hey look at me, look what I can do" type of thing, but it isn't actually practical in real life. I cover this in the 15:10 section of this video.

It's only viable for long, stationary tasks like sorting things or packing e-commerce orders. Yes, you can technically watch a video while washing the dishes, but if you need to turn around and put the dishes away or do something else, it becomes more of a chore to move and adjust the screen than the dishes themselves.

You'd be better off listening to a podcast without the Quest 3, IMO.

author: @maxzeblade

text:
I appreciate that! A driving factor of this channel is transparency and honesty with my reviews so it's good to hear that it's well accepted.

It's hard to tell how Meta's VR play will turn out in the long term - I agree there's a lot of money going into it, but that money is necessary when betting on a new technology. I also think that while their output numbers might seem bad, there's no doubt they're making up costs on the back-end by gathering user data and improving the technology. Plus, by forcing competitors like the Pico 5 out of the market due to margins other companies can't manage, they gain more market share and control of the ecosystem. It's very likely they recover in the future, especially as they now have cameras on multiple devices, but still a gamble.

I definitely think they oversold its capabilities and features - but again, I don't know of a company that doesn't do that.

text:
Wow. Best review and deep dive I've watched! Great information that didn't push me off the fence either way - definitely helped me a LOT. Fantastic dive into the hardware and software from multiple angles that was really insightful. Subscribed!

Question: Did BoboVR fix the issues with their batteries on the updated headset for the Quest 3 that people were complaining about early on? Any issues with your headset?
author: @HelloNNNewman

text:
I truly appreciate that! Thank you for taking the time to watch. =)

No upgrades to the battery yet that I've seen. I believe the issue folks had was that the batteries were the same as used on their Quest 2 headstrap and rated around 10-13w output but the Quest 3 maxes out at 27w. I honestly haven't found it to be an issue; typically when playing a standalone game my Quest 3 is only intaking 10-12w. Even when "partially idle" (off my head, but the screens are still on) it seems like the Quest 3's internals limit how much juice it can intake when the headset is powered on (I'm presuming to reduce heat generated in the headset? but IDK). It only hits that 15-27w range when the device fully idle or if it's not using the internals heavily (like streaming video from my PC).

In most experiences, the BoboVR battery is still being drained before it touches the Quest 3 - there are some instances where that isn't the case and the BoboVR rather extends it. Either way, the battery has doubled my play time and has been 100% worth it (they haven't given me any free products or pay me to say that). I haven't had any real complaints but I presume someone trying to min-max their game session time might. For $45 you really can't go wrong with it lol

That said, if you are on the battery swap fence, Kiwi is sending me their new battery straps which aren't released yet and should be here in the next week and 1 other company is also sending theirs. I bought the Bobo with my own money because so many in the VR community recommended it. I am going to make a review comparing the 3 and against a standard Anker power bank.

author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
I've seen about a dozen general reviews of the Quest 3 and over 100 partial reviews that focus on certain aspects of the Quest 3 and I have to say that this is most thorough and fair review I've seen.

I would like to disagree on one thing though. The PianoVision app is probably the most practical and beneficial MR app on the market if you focus on its main features. Single-handedly this app has legitimized mixed reality for me because it makes something easier to do, rather than it feeling like a gimmick or something that's better on a flat screen.

However, to your credit, this absolutely does NOT include the fake virtual keyboard that's included which is essentially useless unless you just want to see where notes are. That being said, you actually don't need a MIDI keyboard to learn with this app, you can learn with any keyboard or piano.

MIDI keyboards add extra scoring and statistical features to the app but the value in simply knowing exactly where the notes are for any song, laid out on a piano as you play, can't be understated. There are keyboards on the market with keys that light up but none have the ability to show you well in advance what to expect.

The hand-tracking isn't perfect either but again, isn't necessary to learn because you can see your hands and the piano itself will obviously register what key you're hitting. The importance of the app rest mostly in the visual overlay that can show you anything from the notes of a song, to chords, scales, finger exercises, and even what finger you should use to hit a particular key. The app itself includes a large library of songs and exercises but you can also download additional content for free from a range of online sources.

There are quite a few imperfections with this app for sure but thankfully there's no reason it cant continue to be updated to perform better with current hardware. Preferably the Quest 3 for those who have it.

Amazing video other than that one tiny portion of it, in my opinion anyway.
author: @mikem2949

text:
What a fantastic review.

I've just picked up a Q3 as 1st timer to VVR and agree with everything you've said in this video as a vet.

My extra thoughts as a newbie:

When you get it, immediately buy resident evil 4. It's the best game I've found so far for the headset. It's on the headset itself, performs silky smooth, has fantastic IK and holster systems. Just generally does the core VR experience very well.

Anyway, this tech is total fucking pain in the ass lol. That's VR in general, not specific to the Q3. Getting this running as a newbie reminded me of trying to insall windows 2000 from floppy disks as a kid. I made a HUGE mistake making my first games fallout and skyrim, which both require masters degrees in bullshittery to get running.

When set up, there's also a lot of steps between you and playing a game. If you want to play a game with a spectator (I rarely game alone), you have to start streaming on the headset to chrome, then wirelessly connect to your PC, then fire up steam then the steam VR room, then the VR game, then you wait for 6 months while it loads up, then if it survives the crash, you have a good time. But lord, what a faff. If you need to take the headset off for a second to do something IRL, it's super easy to hit buttons on it while taking it off, or press triggers on the handholds by mistake. This can cause apps to suspend, which sometimes makes then crash. The same with walking out of the boundry. Overall, it feels like I'm working with a dev prototype when playing PCVR games and I need to be very careful, not great.

The buying experience for the standalonw games is insanly bad. I tried to buy a music pack for beat saber in the app. Rather than a one-click purchase, you have to start buying it in app, then take your headset off, then get your phone out, then go back and forth between the meta app and your bank, then enter a pin, to get the game. It was 24 clicks in total. wtf. The CX director should have tomatoes thrown at them by angry peasants.

I've used it wired and wirelessly. I have no idea how people played with this with a wire for so many years. Being wireless is a MUST for me if I want to feel immersed in a game now.

The binocular effect kinda sucks, but a good game with a big sense of scale will help with this a lot. Then again, if I got the big picture, my PC couldn't handle games at those resolutions.

3rd party devices are a must to play with it. The headstrap and grips.

I think it was worth it, because the cost isn't that crazy to me. But compare this to a console, that will give you infinately more value for money.

text:
I truly appreciate that!

I haven't played RE4 yet but it's on my list to grab during this sale per your recommendation. I also think it won a GOTY award on a stream recently.

And LOL. I definitely understand the pain getting set up for PCVR. I made the same mistake trying to get Assetto Corsa up and running as my first experience and was nearly tearing my hair out. I ended up bailing and testing out other things first.. luckily stuff like HL:Alyx 'just worked'. I'm plagued with the same issue in that once you need a break to take the headset off, when you come back the positioning/tracking is off or the game can't load back in.

Wireless makes a MASSIVE difference, IMO, and is what makes the Quest 3 is so compelling in the sea of VR/AR devices. Even basic movements like turning around with cable attached to your head is basically impossible without setting up a custom pulley rig and I've nearly yanked my motherboard out of the case and the headset off my face getting the cable trapped even while sitting stationary. Even with the XREAL Air 2 Pros, just having the cable into your pocket, which is still attached to you, is a pain to deal with.

I agree that it was worth it - I am admittedly not using it as much as I hoped I would. It has literally been sitting on my desk here since I published this review.. so a full 11 days without touching it and I've only felt the want to put it on because of Asgard's Wrath 2. The trade-off of eye strain hasn't yet found a balance in my work life; by end of day when I am ready to wind down, my eyes are exhausted and putting on the Quest 3 feels like a chore.

text:
This is a very good review of the Quest 3 and covers lots of points I have personally felt.

One thing I wanted to mention was that your comment at 11:30 into the video about controller latency seems to be slightly inaccurate, if I am understanding you correctly. While the illusion is definitely broken in pass-through, I don't think it's controller latency. That tracking is still very low lat, but the video pass-through is higher latency however.
Notice how in your video, the virtual controller is slightly ahead of the video version of the controller. Your controllers are tracked very quickly, however the video is being post processed (from 2 16MP cameras downscaled to 4MP btw) to appear like the image is recorded from your eyes perspective.

I find this worth mentioning because this high (relatively speaking) latency of pass-through would make me feel a bit "odd", for a couple of hours after VR. My hands in the real world didn't quite feel my own. But after a few weeks i stopped getting that effect.
Also it's worth keeping in mind that the controllers are very well tracked for games that require very quick response times.

Edit: I am now further into the video and see that you've covered the camera specs. Hopefully I don't have to retract my entire comment by the end of the video 😅
author: @_Huffy

text:
I truly appreciate that!

You may be correct about passthrough controller latency. In my eyes, what was happening was the software was "pre-determining" the position of the virtual controllers through an algorithm, and simply placing them where it "predicted" them to be next, because it couldn't possibly keep up in real time (I realize now I said the opposite in the video trying to explain it). However, your theory of the passthrough camera lag is equally compelling. I will test more and e-mail Meta support to see if they can explain for us!

I agree it feels 'odd' in MR. Using my theory, it made it seem like the same prediction algorithm is probably occurring in virtual games, we just don't notice it because we can't see it - and therefore the VR version FEELS accurate because we have re-adjusted our perspective to that prediction.

I definitely agree that the tracking otherwise feels fine and seems super accurate in VR, even down to miniscule movements of pointing! I have 0 complaints in that department (outside a few incorrect tracking instances when trying to reload guns in certain games).

I haven't been able to get used to it MR thus far, but that may be due to a lack of engaging experience. I will have to download some newer MR experiences when they come out and try to get immersed with them to see if I can get accustomed to it like you have.
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
​@@aline.tech.reviewsthanks for the response. I'll be interested to hear your further thoughts if you get to test it more (and if you hear back from support).

Something that struck me straight away was I sent several text messages on my phone, through the headset pass-through. Then after a few minutes I took it off and tried writing another message on my phone. I instantly felt like my hands weren't my own!

text:
I have upgraded from a quest 2 to the 3. I feel that overall, the biggest improvement between the 2 is the processing power of the new chipset. From things such as rendering speeds, interacting with menus, and being able to run more intense beatsaber mods to be able to play certain custom maps it is by far the biggest difference I have noticed. The second being the sweet spot of the upgraded lenses. Much more FOV allows me to use my eyes to glance at my surroundings.

I will say that the resolution upgrade itself doesn't feel substantial from the quest 2 and that overall, you can get most of the same experiences through the quest 2. MR is as stated in the video, very early stage and is overpromised.

I would definitely say that getting a secondhand quest 2 is not a bad choice, at least until we see more consumer devices pop onto the market.
author: @leek_san

text:
Agree with that conclusion!

The resolution, especially, was a big hit to me once I realized the headset is running at a ~20% reduction in standalone mode and the PPD, while 25% higher, didn't make a huge difference - wondering if it's a potentially marketing thing of the term "peak" in "peak pixel density", and overall the screen is much less. I'm not experienced enough with VR displays to guess one way or the other. It destroyed my hopes of using it all day, every day, as my main monitor replacement for work.

Just yesterday, however, I got an e-mail from Meta that updated developers with some new tools and a breakdown of how their First Encounters game was able to break meshes - and inclusion of occlusion updates for hiding VR objects behind IRL objects. If developers start pushing out neat experiences, then MR may be able to catch up to its promise.
author: @aline.tech.reviews

text:
@@aline.tech.reviews I think that my main concern with MR is that it isn't at the point where it can be done reliably for the mainstream market. The mix of graininess, warping, and simply not being anywhere close to 60 ppd (60 being close to the amount a human eye cannot discern pixels at the distance) leaves a bitter experience.

On one hand, you are left in awe seeing the improvements of MR technology and the upgrade between the quest 2 cameras but on the flipside, you know that the hardware and software simply isn't good enough to futureproof and allow room for iteration. It also sucks thinking about the fact that these improved cameras and included depth sensor is probably the culprit of the biggest power draws from the quest 3 which would definitely explain the hours of playtime between quest 2.

No matter how many workarounds and problem solving against these clear issues, I cannot see the quest 3 being a premiere MR device despite what it is marketed for.

I think that MR needs more funding towards research and more money going towards better hardware even if it makes the end product less consumer friendly regarding the budget. This way, we can have these advancements eventually trickle down just like smartphones. All of the above leads to why I am excited to see Apple's Vision headset, as they clearly aren't messing around with the price it is going to be sold at.

Aside from my rambling, I am happy with the direction you took with your video. It is very grounded and definitely describes the product very accurately without bias or influence from hype. Keep up the good work, I can definitely see your content being on the same stage as other VR/MR content creators :)

author: @BouncingCupCake-w8g

text:
For A: It depends on the design of your glasses, but I think if you have standard-ish frames then they will fit fine - the face shield is wide enough and adjusts out to accommodate what I presume is a "standard" pair of glasses (just don't forget to actually put it at the highest setting before you put the headset on). I have driving glasses and they fit fine, but I do find it uncomfortable as they are pressing in on the sides of my face more.

I would say it's going to be a better, and safer experience, to just save up for the lenses. Zenni has them for $50.

For B: You will probably want to search Reddit to see how people are faring; I am seeing mixed results. I don't have experience since I don't wear glasses regularly.

The straps on the back of the default headstrap feel strong enough that you would be able to jerry-rig something on the backside and stay comfortable. I think manually mounting it to the back will give you a better weight balance and comfort than the top of your head.. otherwise, get a long USB cable and put it down the back of your shirt into a pants pocket.

text: Do you think this would be a good upgrade from the valve index in terms of PCVR gaming?
author: @Icequake.

text:
Very subjective based on needs - but I would vote yes unless you need full body tracking for fun times.

The visual clarity is miles ahead (optics, better sweet spot, higher resolution, and increased PPD), you don't need tracking stations, and you can go wireless (which is amazing with AV1 codec, but you can also still be tethered if you want).

It's less comfortable, the wireless battery life isn't great, and the FOV/audio are less quality than the Index.

Valve hasn't announced any news on an Index 2/Deckard even after their Steam Link + VR Fest weeks.. so I'm not sure if waiting would be better.

If you were comparing buying an Index vs a Quest 3 today, it would be the Quest 3 hands down. But upgrading is more subjective and will be up to you. The Index is selling used for $600 which would cover the cost of your upgrade - that seems like a good trade IMO.

text:
As an early adopter of the HTC vive - and now Quest 3 - 7 years later, I'd say you're the most honest reviewer there is about this. Everything you said lines perfectly up with my 2-weeks with the headset (I bought on a whim). I opted for the 512 GB version since I could already figure out that an increased resolution means bigger apps, and already I've used over half the space of your 128 GB version.

There is ONE glaring difference between all other VR headsets that sets this one appart, and that is the wireless functionality. Not being hardware thethered to the PC is a HUGE plus, and undervaluated plus no one seems to mention. Since I am coming from a ridicoulus 1st gen setup with low resolution, fresnel lenses, super-accurate but clumsy lighthouse settings, and a setup that demands 3 power supplies, messing around with setting up HDMI (which windows often mistakes for a second screen) and all the cables, not to mention having to un-twirl yourself from the huge thick cable behind your neck - to a lightweight headset you can just put on, connect, and you're there - done! This is a viable option for me. I am already using it more than I was using my original HTC Vive 7 years ago (which has gathered dust since then).

The battery life is indeed frustrating, but as you said - it kinda becomes a blessing in disguise, because you DO need rest after using it, being in VR wirelessly is a high-intensity experience that takes a toll on the mind and the eyes, so for now - I think that's kind of okay.

Alyx was mind-blowing with this, it already was with the HTC, but the details with the better resolution, wider-field, clearer lenses made me sit for 30 minutes and just stare at electrical outlets, walls, and NPCS in full-body size around me with total awe. However, even having a totally super-high end PC + probably the best Wifi around, it still suffer from the occasional breakup, meaning it will go from crystal clarity to "mpeg-compression-stutter" albeit it usually just last a second or two, but enough to be annoying.

Meta should have opted for a Dual-Router / Dual connection Wifi here, meaning you could connect to eg. two 5 Ghz routers at the same time, that would have solved these issues, because despite having Wifi 6 (which is in theory capable of pushing through several gigabits) you'll still end up around 300-400 mbps which sadly is NOT enough to push even 75-120 hz (frames per second) with a total resolution of 4.5K because that's gonna demand INSANE bandwidth. Dual router/dual connection would solve this as even with Wifi 6E, your signal will fade away and the throughput just wont reach you, unless you have double modems, the speed is severely "physically" restrained and limited by your environment/walls/interference etc. That's why a 5Ghz vs a Wifi 6 router won't make much of a difference, something NO ONE talks about.
author: @joonglegamer9898

text:
I appreciate you taking the time to watch and leave such a thoughtful comment!

RE: storage - I'm already at 80%+ utilization and I won't be able to fit Asgard's Wrath on Friday; I will have to remove things (admittedly, I installed a bunch of crap I won't use much for this review). It's not deal breaker for me as I don't imagine playing standalone games often.. but there's no doubt it will become a hassle once future games are released and I want to preserve my local game saves or capture video. On that note, I have had a lot of frustration getting the Quest 3 to show up in Windows so I can access it as a file storage device - the headset requires me to look through the lenses and accept a prompt EVERY time and will disconnect if I'm too rough with setting it down like the USB-C connection is fragile. I raged out quite a few times trying to get footage from the headset to my PC for this review, and ended up using a lot of stock video instead because it was wasting so much of my time. I would imagine most users would prefer the 512GB model to not have to deal with the continuous uninstalling/re-installing dance.

RE: wireless - couldn't agree more and the experience you described with the previous HMD is the content of my nightmares.. that, mixed with the tech demo-esque vibe of most VR experiences prior to the Quest 2 era, is why I hesitated jumping into the VR waters for so long. The ability to go wireless PCVR + completely standalone are the primary reasons I chose the Quest 3 over the Bigscreen Beyond. And now, having tried the Quest 3 both wired and wirelessly, I fear I'm going to have a hard time going back to the wired-only Immersed Visor when that arrives. Immersed claimed it will have an onboard battery as of recent, but it's only meant to let you get up and stretch your legs or unplug for very minimal sprints.

RE: battery life - same page here; with the BoboVR M3 Pro I can get through the majority of a basic work day, which I feel is enough and allows me to use my laptop from anywhere in my home. As you alluded, I typically feel a exhausted after a longer work sprint and the breaks to recharge are necessary. I would like to see fast(er) charging for those times I'd like to set it down for a quick break and get back in.

RE: HL Alyx - It's insane. I've read a lot of hype around VR headsets, experiences, games, use cases, and so on.. but I'd venture to say this was the only one that lived up to it. I believe the game is only ~12 hours long and I'm pretty sure it's going to take me 3x that because I have to bend down to look at and pick up everything.

RE: wifi - I purchased a dedicated router for the Quest 3 get the same compression spikes. Honestly, your proposed solution is one I wouldn't have thought of and wasn't sure was possible. I wonder if the reason for not doing that is because of the logistics nightmare + processing required to sync it all on the headset's side? I'm not familiar enough with wireless functionality to be pondering or questioning too deeply lololol.

I assumed a dedicated Wifi 5 router would hit speeds 700mbps-1gbps (7gbps theoretical). You mentioned no one's talking about it which makes it seem like a fun test for a YT video. I presume we could use the VD monitoring tools to log the outcomes.

PCVR doesn't eat space. But videos do, and a lot. That's another thing to consider. And yeah, for some people 4 big games at once is not enough. That's what I get in Playstation 5 though :)

author: @jaumededios484

text:
What do you do in AR on the piano? If you're planning to use an app like PianoVision, then it's probably not worth the upgrade. It's effectively "Guitar Hero" but for the piano (it simply drops notes and you have to hit them on time"), and doesn't work well unless you have a MIDI keyboard connected directly to the Quest 3 headset. The virtual keyboard, if you don't have a MIDI keyboard, does NOT work well enough with hand tracking to be useful.

RE: Quest 2 vs. 3, it's worth the upgrade if you absolutely need the upgraded cameras for AR/XR experiences - the cameras are no doubt better (just keep in mind it's still blurry - reminiscent of an old 720p webcam).. or if you use your Quest 2 a lot and want the upgraded visuals. Otherwise, I'd stick with the Quest 2.
